**Kenya**,[[a]](#cite_note-13) officially the **Republic of Kenya**,[[b]](#cite_note-14) is a country located in [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa"). With an estimated population of more than 53.3 million as of mid-2025,[[13]](#cite_note-15) Kenya is the [27th-most populous country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population") in the world[[7]](#cite_note-KNBS2019-7) and the [seventh-most populous](/wiki/List_of_African_countries_by_population "List of African countries by population") in Africa. Kenya's capital and largest city is [Nairobi](/wiki/Nairobi "Nairobi"). The second-largest and oldest city is [Mombasa](/wiki/Mombasa "Mombasa"), a major port city located on [Mombasa Island](/wiki/Mombasa_Island "Mombasa Island"). Other major cities within the country include [Kisumu](/wiki/Kisumu "Kisumu"), [Nakuru](/wiki/Nakuru "Nakuru") and [Eldoret](/wiki/Eldoret "Eldoret"). Going clockwise, Kenya is bordered by [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan") to the northwest (though much of that border includes the disputed [Ilemi Triangle](/wiki/Ilemi_Triangle "Ilemi Triangle")), [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia") to the north, [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia") to the east, the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") to the southeast, [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania") to the southwest, and [Lake Victoria](/wiki/Lake_Victoria "Lake Victoria") and [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda") to the west.

Kenya's [geography](/wiki/Geography_of_Kenya "Geography of Kenya"), [climate](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Kenya "Climate change in Kenya") and [population](/wiki/Demographics_of_Kenya "Demographics of Kenya") vary widely. In western Rift Valley counties, the landscape includes cold, snow-capped mountaintops (such as Batian, Nelion, and Point Lenana on [Mount Kenya](/wiki/Mount_Kenya "Mount Kenya")) with vast surrounding forests, wildlife, and fertile agricultural regions in temperate climates. In other areas there are dry, [arid](/wiki/Arid "Arid"), and [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") climates, as well as absolute deserts (such as the [Chalbi Desert](/wiki/Chalbi_Desert "Chalbi Desert") and [Nyiri Desert](/wiki/Nyiri_Desert "Nyiri Desert")).

Kenya's earliest inhabitants included some of the first humans to evolve from ancestral members of the genus [Homo](/wiki/Homo "Homo"). Ample fossil evidence for this evolutionary history has been found at [Koobi Fora](/wiki/Koobi_Fora "Koobi Fora"). Later, Kenya was inhabited by [hunter-gatherers](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") similar to the present-day [Hadza people](/wiki/Hadza_people "Hadza people").[[14]](#cite_note-16)[[15]](#cite_note-17) According to archaeological dating of associated artifacts and skeletal material, [Cushitic speakers](/wiki/South_Cushitic_languages "South Cushitic languages") first settled in the region's lowlands between 3,200 and 1,300 BC, a phase known as the Lowland [Savanna Pastoral Neolithic](/wiki/Savanna_Pastoral_Neolithic "Savanna Pastoral Neolithic"). [Nilotic](/wiki/Nilo-Saharan_languages "Nilo-Saharan languages")-speaking [pastoralists](/wiki/Pastoralism "Pastoralism") (ancestral to Kenya's Nilotic speakers) began migrating from present-day [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan") into Kenya around 500 BC.[[16]](#cite_note-EhretCHS-18) [Bantu people](/wiki/Bantu_languages "Bantu languages") settled at the coast and the interior between 250 BC and 500 AD.[[17]](#cite_note-19)

European contact began in 1500 AD with the [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"), and effective colonisation of Kenya began in the 19th century during the [European exploration of Africa](/wiki/European_exploration_of_Africa "European exploration of Africa"). Modern-day Kenya emerged from [a protectorate](/wiki/East_Africa_Protectorate "East Africa Protectorate"), established by the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") in 1895, and the subsequent [Kenya Colony](/wiki/Kenya_Colony "Kenya Colony"), which began in 1920. Mombasa was the capital of the [British East Africa Protectorate](/wiki/British_East_Africa_Protectorate "British East Africa Protectorate"),[[18]](#cite_note-20) which included most of what is now Kenya and southwestern Somalia, from 1889 to 1907. Numerous disputes between the UK and the colony led to the [Mau Mau revolution](/wiki/Mau_Mau_revolution "Mau Mau revolution"), which began in 1952, and the declaration of Kenya's independence in 1963. After independence, Kenya remained a member of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"). The country's [current constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Kenya "Constitution of Kenya") was adopted in 2010, replacing the previous [1963 constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Kenya_(1963) "Constitution of Kenya (1963)").

Kenya is a [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") [representative democratic](/wiki/Representative_democratic "Representative democratic") republic, in which elected officials represent the people and the president is the head of state and government.[[19]](#cite_note-21) The country is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), [the Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"), [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), [COMESA](/wiki/COMESA "COMESA"), [International Criminal Court](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court "International Criminal Court"), as well as several other international organisations. It is also a [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

[Kenya's economy](/wiki/Kenya%27s_economy "Kenya's economy") is the largest in East and Central Africa, with Nairobi serving as a major regional commercial hub.[[20]](#cite_note-largest-economy-22) With a per-capita [Gross National Income](/wiki/Gross_National_Income "Gross National Income") of $2,110,[[21]](#cite_note-23) the country is a [lower-middle-income](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") economy. Agriculture is the country's largest economic sector; tea and coffee are the sector's traditional cash crops, while fresh flowers are a fast-growing export. The [service industry](/wiki/Service_industry "Service industry"), particularly tourism, is also one of the country's major economic drivers. Kenya is a member of the [East African Community](/wiki/East_African_Community "East African Community") trade bloc,[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25) though some international trade organisations categorise it as part of the [Greater Horn of Africa](/wiki/Greater_Horn_of_Africa "Greater Horn of Africa").[[24]](#cite_note-26) Africa is Kenya's largest export market, followed by the European Union.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

## Etymology

The Republic of Kenya is named after [Mount Kenya](/wiki/Mount_Kenya "Mount Kenya"). The earliest recorded version of the modern name was written by German explorer [Johann Ludwig Krapf](/wiki/Johann_Ludwig_Krapf "Johann Ludwig Krapf") in the 19th century. While travelling with a Kamba caravan led by the long-distance trader [Chief Kivoi](/wiki/Chief_Kivoi_Mwendwa "Chief Kivoi Mwendwa"), Krapf spotted the mountain peak and asked what it was called. Kivoi told him "*Kĩ-Nyaa*" or "*Kĩlĩma- Kĩinyaa*", probably because the pattern of black rock and white snow on its peaks reminded him of the feathers of the male ostrich.[[26]](#cite_note-sullivan-28) In archaic [Kikuyu](/wiki/Gikuyu_language "Gikuyu language"), the word 'nyaga' or more commonly 'manyaganyaga' is used to describe an extremely bright object. The Agikuyu, who inhabit the slopes of Mt. Kenya, call it Kĩrĩma Kĩrĩnyaga (literally 'the mountain with brightness') in [Kikuyu](/wiki/Kikuyu_language "Kikuyu language"), while the [Embu](/wiki/Embu_language "Embu language") call it "*Ki-nyaga*" or Kirima Kinyaga. All three names have the same meaning.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

Ludwig Krapf recorded the name as both *Kenia* and *Kegnia*.[[28]](#cite_note-Krapf-30)[[29]](#cite_note-krapf_452-31)[[30]](#cite_note-foottit-32) Some have said that this was a precise notation of the African pronunciation [/ˈkɛnjə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English").[[31]](#cite_note-33) An 1882 map drawn by Joseph Thompsons, a Scottish geologist and naturalist, indicated Mt. Kenya as Mt. Kenia.[[26]](#cite_note-sullivan-28) The mountain's name was accepted, *[pars pro toto](/wiki/Pars_pro_toto "Pars pro toto")*, as the name of the country. It did not come into widespread official use during the early colonial period, when the country was referred to as the [East African Protectorate](/wiki/East_Africa_Protectorate "East Africa Protectorate"). The official name was changed to the [Colony of Kenya](/wiki/Kenya_Colony "Kenya Colony") in 1920.

## History

Main article: [History of Kenya](/wiki/History_of_Kenya "History of Kenya")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Kenya](/wiki/Timeline_of_Kenya "Timeline of Kenya").

### Prehistory

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Turkana_Boy.jpg/250px-Turkana_Boy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turkana_Boy.jpg)

[Turkana Boy](/wiki/Turkana_boy "Turkana boy"), a 1.6-million-year-old *[Homo erectus](/wiki/Homo_erectus "Homo erectus")* fossil

[Hominid](/wiki/Hominid "Hominid") species, such as *[Homo habilis](/wiki/Homo_habilis "Homo habilis")* (fl. 1.8 to 2.5 million years ago) and *[Homo erectus](/wiki/Homo_erectus "Homo erectus")* (fl. 1.9 million to 350,000 years ago), possibly the direct ancestors of modern *[Homo sapiens](/wiki/Homo_sapiens "Homo sapiens")*, had lived in Kenya in the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") epoch.[[32]](#cite_note-Isaac-34) East Africa, including Kenya, is one of the earliest regions where modern humans (*[Homo sapiens](/wiki/Homo_sapiens "Homo sapiens")*) are believed to have lived. In 1984, during excavations at Lake Turkana palaeoanthropologist [Richard Leakey](/wiki/Richard_Leakey "Richard Leakey"), assisted by [Kamoya Kimeu](/wiki/Kamoya_Kimeu "Kamoya Kimeu"), had discovered the [Turkana Boy](/wiki/Turkana_Boy "Turkana Boy"), a 1.6-million-year-old *Homo erectus* fossil. Further evidence of Kenya's prehistory was found in 2018, namely the early emergence of [modern behaviours](/wiki/Behavioral_modernity "Behavioral modernity"), including long-distance trade networks (involving goods such as [obsidian](/wiki/Obsidian "Obsidian")), the use of pigments, and possibly the making of [projectile points](/wiki/Projectile_points "Projectile points"), about 320,000 years ago. The authors of three 2018 studies on the site suggest that complex and modern behaviours had already begun in Africa around the time of the emergence of *Homo sapiens*.[[33]](#cite_note-NPR-593591796-35)[[34]](#cite_note-The_Atlantic-555674-36)[[35]](#cite_note-Brooks-37)

The first inhabitants of present-day Kenya were ancestral hunter-gatherer groups, akin to the modern Khoisan speakers. They were subsequently joined by Southern Nilotic hunter-gatherers, such as the [Ogiek](/wiki/Okiek_people "Okiek people"), who established a deep, indigenous presence in the highland forests long before the arrival of later migrating groups.[[36]](#cite_note-38)[[37]](#cite_note-EhretC18-39) These people were later largely replaced by agropastoralist [Cushitic](/wiki/Cushitic "Cushitic") (ancestral to Kenya's Cushitic speakers), who originated from the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa").[[38]](#cite_note-EhretHRSC-40) During the early [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene"), the region's climate shifted from drier to wetter conditions. This provided an opportunity for the development of cultural traditions such as agriculture and [herding](/wiki/Herding "Herding") in a more favourable environment.[[37]](#cite_note-EhretC18-39)

[Nilotic-speaking](/wiki/Nilotic_peoples "Nilotic peoples") pastoralists began migrating into Kenya from the [Nile Valley](/wiki/Nile_Valley "Nile Valley") and the [Ethiopian Highlands](/wiki/Ethiopian_Highlands "Ethiopian Highlands") as early as 2000 BC. The earliest arrivals were [Southern Nilotes](/wiki/Southern_Nilotes "Southern Nilotes") (ancestors of the [Kalenjin](/wiki/Kalenjin_people "Kalenjin people")), followed by subsequent waves of Eastern and Western Nilotes.[[39]](#cite_note-41)[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[16]](#cite_note-EhretCHS-18)[[41]](#cite_note-AmbroseALR-43)[[42]](#cite_note-AmbroseSG-44) Today, the country's Nilotic ethnic groups include the [Kalenjin](/wiki/Kalenjin_people "Kalenjin people"), [Samburu](/wiki/Samburu_people "Samburu people"), [Luo](/wiki/Luo_(family_of_ethnic_groups) "Luo (family of ethnic groups)"), [Turkana](/wiki/Turkana_people "Turkana people"), [Maasai](/wiki/Maasai_people "Maasai people") and others.[[43]](#cite_note-ILO-45)

By the first millennium AD, [Bantu](/wiki/Bantu_languages "Bantu languages")-speaking farmers had moved into the region, initially along the Kenyan coast.[[44]](#cite_note-EhretACA-46) The Bantus had originated in West Africa along the [Benue River](/wiki/Benue_River "Benue River") in what is now eastern [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") and western [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon").[[45]](#cite_note-WayneSmith-47) The [Bantu migration](/wiki/Bantu_migration "Bantu migration") brought new developments in agriculture and [ironworking](/wiki/History_of_ferrous_metallurgy "History of ferrous metallurgy") to the region.[[45]](#cite_note-WayneSmith-47) Today, the country's Bantu groups include the [Kikuyu](/wiki/Kikuyu_people "Kikuyu people"), [Luhya](/wiki/Luhya_people "Luhya people"), [Kamba](/wiki/Kamba_people "Kamba people"), [Kisii](/wiki/Gusii_people "Gusii people"), [Meru](/wiki/Meru_people "Meru people"), [Kuria](/wiki/Kuria_people "Kuria people"), [Aembu](/wiki/Aembu "Aembu"), [Ambeere](/wiki/Ambeere "Ambeere"), [Wadawida](/wiki/Wadawida "Wadawida")-Watuweta, Wapokomo, and [Mijikenda](/wiki/Mijikenda_peoples "Mijikenda peoples"), among many others.

Notable prehistoric sites in the interior of Kenya include the (possibly archaeoastronomical) site [Namoratunga](/wiki/Namoratunga "Namoratunga") on the west side of [Lake Turkana](/wiki/Lake_Turkana "Lake Turkana") and the walled settlement of [Thimlich Ohinga](/wiki/Thimlich_Ohinga "Thimlich Ohinga") in [Migori County](/wiki/Migori_County "Migori County").

### Swahili trade period

Further information: [Swahili culture](/wiki/Swahili_culture "Swahili culture") and [Sultanate of Zanzibar](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Zanzibar "Sultanate of Zanzibar")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Lamu_door.jpg/250px-Lamu_door.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lamu_door.jpg)

A traditional [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_culture "Swahili culture") carved wooden door in [Lamu](/wiki/Lamu "Lamu")

The coastline of Kenya was home to communities of [ironworkers](/wiki/Ironwork "Ironwork") and Bantu subsistence farmers, hunters, and fishers who supported the region's economy with agriculture, fishing, metal production, and trade with foreign countries. These communities formed the earliest [city-states](/wiki/City-states "City-states") in the region, which were collectively known as [Azania](/wiki/Azania "Azania").[[46]](#cite_note-pbs.org-48) The Swahili people were of mixed African and Asian (particularly Persian) ancestry, as [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") evidence has revealed.[[47]](#cite_note-49)

By the 1st century CE, many of the area's city-states, such as [Mombasa](/wiki/Mombasa "Mombasa"), [Malindi](/wiki/Malindi "Malindi"), and [Zanzibar](/wiki/Zanzibar "Zanzibar"), began to establish trading relations with the [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs"). This led to increased economic growth of the Swahili states, the introduction of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") influences on the [Swahili language](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language"), [cultural diffusion](/wiki/Cultural_diffusion "Cultural diffusion"), as well as the Swahili city-states becoming members of a larger trade network.[[48]](#cite_note-50)[[49]](#cite_note-Nanjira-51) Many historians had long believed that the city-states were established by Arab or Persian traders, but archaeological evidence has led scholars to recognise the city-states as an indigenous development which, though subjected to foreign influence due to trade, retained a Bantu cultural core.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

The [Kilwa Sultanate](/wiki/Kilwa_Sultanate "Kilwa Sultanate") was a medieval [sultanate](/wiki/Sultanate "Sultanate") centred at [Kilwa](/wiki/Kilwa_Kisiwani "Kilwa Kisiwani"), in modern-day Tanzania. At its height, its authority stretched over the entire length of the [Swahili Coast](/wiki/Swahili_Coast "Swahili Coast"), including Kenya.[[51]](#cite_note-53) Beginning in the 10th century, the rulers of Kilwa would go on to build elaborate coral mosques and introduce copper coinage.[[52]](#cite_note-54)

Swahili, a [Bantu language](/wiki/Bantu_language "Bantu language") with [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"), [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), and other Middle-Eastern and South Asian [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword"), later developed as a *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* for trade between the different peoples.[[46]](#cite_note-pbs.org-48) Since the turn of the 20th century, Swahili has also adopted numerous loanwords and [calques](/wiki/Calque "Calque") from English, many of which originated during the era of British colonial rule.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

### Early Portuguese presence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Braun_Mombasa_UBHD.jpg/250px-Braun_Mombasa_UBHD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Braun_Mombasa_UBHD.jpg)

[Portuguese presence](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") in Kenya lasted from 1498 until 1730. [Mombasa](/wiki/Mombasa "Mombasa") was under Portuguese rule from 1593 to 1698 and again from 1728 to 1729.

The Swahili built Mombasa into a major port city and established trade links with other nearby city-states, as well as commercial centres in Persia, Arabia, and even India.[[54]](#cite_note-56) By the 15th century, Portuguese voyager [Duarte Barbosa](/wiki/Duarte_Barbosa "Duarte Barbosa") wrote that "Mombasa is a place of great traffic and has a good harbour in which there are always moored small craft of many kinds and also great ships, both of which are bound from [Sofala](/wiki/Sofala "Sofala") and others which come from [Cambay](/wiki/Cambay "Cambay") and [Melinde](/wiki/Malindi "Malindi") and others which sail to the island of Zanzibar."[[55]](#cite_note-57)

One major city on the Kenyan coast is Malindi. It has been an important Swahili settlement since the 14th century, and the city once rivalled Mombasa for dominance in the [African Great Lakes](/wiki/African_Great_Lakes "African Great Lakes") region. Malindi has traditionally been a friendly port city for foreign powers. In 1414, the Chinese trader and explorer [Zheng He](/wiki/Zheng_He "Zheng He"), representing the [Ming Dynasty](/wiki/Ming_Dynasty "Ming Dynasty"), visited the East African coast on one of his last '[treasure voyages](/wiki/Ming_treasure_voyages "Ming treasure voyages")'.[[56]](#cite_note-58) Malindi also welcomed the Portuguese explorer [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama "Vasco da Gama") in 1498.

In the 17th century, the Swahili coast was conquered by the [Omani Arabs](/wiki/Omani_Arabs "Omani Arabs"), who expanded the [slave trade](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_slave_trade "Indian Ocean slave trade") to meet the demands of plantations in [Oman](/wiki/Oman "Oman") and Zanzibar.[[57]](#cite_note-59) Initially, these traders came mainly from Oman, but later many came from Zanzibar (such as [Tippu Tip](/wiki/Tippu_Tip "Tippu Tip")).[[58]](#cite_note-60) In addition, the Portuguese started buying slaves from the Omani and Zanzibari traders in response to the interruption of the [transatlantic slave trade](/wiki/Transatlantic_slave_trade "Transatlantic slave trade") by British abolitionists.

### 18th and 19th centuries

During the 18th and 19th century, the [Masai people](/wiki/Masai_people "Masai people") moved into the central and southern [Rift Valley](/wiki/Great_Rift_Valley,_Kenya "Great Rift Valley, Kenya") plains of Kenya, from a region north of Lake Rudolf (now [Lake Turkana](/wiki/Lake_Turkana "Lake Turkana")). Although there weren't many of them, they managed to conquer a great amount of land in the plains, where people did not put up much resistance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Nandi](/wiki/Nandi_people "Nandi people") peoples managed to oppose the Masai, while the Taveta peoples fled to the forests on the eastern edge of [Mount Kilimanjaro](/wiki/Mount_Kilimanjaro "Mount Kilimanjaro"), although they later were forced to leave the land due to the threat of [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"). An outbreak of either [rinderpest](/wiki/Rinderpest "Rinderpest") or [pleuropneumonia](/wiki/Pleuropneumonia "Pleuropneumonia") greatly affected the Masai's cattle, while an epidemic of smallpox affected the Masai themselves. After the death of the Masai [Mbatian](/wiki/Mbatian "Mbatian"), the chief *laibon* (medicine man), the Masai split into warring factions. The Masai caused much strife in the areas they conquered; however, cooperation between such groups as the [Luo people](/wiki/Luo_people "Luo people"), [Luhya people](/wiki/Luhya_people "Luhya people"), and [Gusii people](/wiki/Gusii_people "Gusii people") is evidenced by shared vocabulary for modern implements and similar economic regimes.[[59]](#cite_note-61) Although Arab traders remained in the area, trade routes were disrupted by the hostile Masai, though there was trade in ivory between these factions.[[60]](#cite_note-62)

The first foreigners to successfully get past the Masai were [Johann Ludwig Krapf](/wiki/Johann_Ludwig_Krapf "Johann Ludwig Krapf") and [Johannes Rebmann](/wiki/Johannes_Rebmann "Johannes Rebmann"), two German missionaries who established a mission in [Rabai](/wiki/Rabai "Rabai"), not too far from [Mombasa](/wiki/Mombasa "Mombasa"). The pair were the first Europeans to sight [Mount Kenya](/wiki/Mount_Kenya "Mount Kenya").[[61]](#cite_note-63)

### German Protectorate (1885–1890)

The [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") established a protectorate over the [Sultan of Zanzibar](/wiki/Sultan_of_Zanzibar "Sultan of Zanzibar")'s coastal possessions in 1885, followed by the arrival of the [Imperial British East Africa Company](/wiki/Imperial_British_East_Africa_Company "Imperial British East Africa Company") in 1888. Imperial rivalry was prevented by the [Heligoland–Zanzibar Treaty](/wiki/Heligoland%E2%80%93Zanzibar_Treaty "Heligoland–Zanzibar Treaty"), so Germany handed its East African coastal holdings to Britain in 1890.

### Nandi Resistance (1890–1906)

Main article: [Nandi Resistance](/wiki/Nandi_Resistance "Nandi Resistance")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Kenyan_National_Library_Service_visit_during_Wikimania_2025_20.jpg/250px-Kenyan_National_Library_Service_visit_during_Wikimania_2025_20.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kenyan_National_Library_Service_visit_during_Wikimania_2025_20.jpg)

Sculpture of [Koitalel Arap Samoei](/wiki/Koitalel_Arap_Samoei "Koitalel Arap Samoei") in the Kenya National Library Service building.

The **Nandi Resistance** (1890–1906), *also known as the Kalenjin Resistance*, was the most prolonged military challenge to British [colonial establishment](/wiki/Colonial_expansion "Colonial expansion") in [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa"). For nearly two decades, the [Nandi](/wiki/Nandi_people "Nandi people") and [Kipsigis](/wiki/Kipsigis_people "Kipsigis people") peoples—led by the Orkoiyot [Koitalel Arap Samoei](/wiki/Koitalel_Arap_Samoei "Koitalel Arap Samoei") defended their territories in the western highlands of [Kenya's Rift Valley](/wiki/Kenya_Rift_Valley "Kenya Rift Valley"). By utilizing effective [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") and sabotage against the construction of the [Uganda Railway](/wiki/Uganda_Railway "Uganda Railway"), they successfully stalled British administrative control for nearly two decades.[[62]](#cite_note-64)[[63]](#cite_note-65)

Despite several "punitive expeditions," the British were unable to achieve a conventional military victory over the Kalenjin warriors. The resistance was broken through an act of [treachery](/wiki/Betrayal "Betrayal") on October 19, 1905, when [Colonel Richard Meinertzhagen](/wiki/Richard_Meinertzhagen "Richard Meinertzhagen") lured Samoei to a purported truce meeting and assassinated him. Following this, Samoei's body was [decapitated](/wiki/Decapitated "Decapitated") and his head was taken to [London](/wiki/London "London") as a colonial trophy, marking the end of the fierce military opposition by the Kalenjin and allowing the British to finally establish administrative control over the region.[[64]](#cite_note-66)[[65]](#cite_note-67)

### British Kenya (1888–1962)

Main article: [Kenya Colony](/wiki/Kenya_Colony "Kenya Colony")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Africa_1909_16a.png/250px-Africa_1909_16a.png)](/wiki/File:Africa_1909_16a.png)

[British East Africa](/wiki/British_East_Africa "British East Africa") in 1909

The transfer by Germany to Britain was followed by the building of the [Uganda Railway](/wiki/Uganda_Railway "Uganda Railway") passing through the country.[[66]](#cite_note-Encyclopædia_Britannica-68)

The building of the railway was resisted by some ethnic groups—notably the [Nandi](/wiki/Nandi_people "Nandi people"), led by *[Orkoiyot](/wiki/Orkoiyot "Orkoiyot")* [Koitalel Arap Samoei](/wiki/Koitalel_Arap_Samoei "Koitalel Arap Samoei") from 1890 to 1900—but the British eventually built it. The *Nandi* were the first ethnic group to be put in a native reserve to stop them from disrupting the building of the railway.[[66]](#cite_note-Encyclopædia_Britannica-68)

During the railway construction era, there was a significant influx of Indian workers, who provided the bulk of the skilled labour required for construction.[[67]](#cite_note-R._Mugo_Gatheru_2005-69) They and most of their descendants later remained in Kenya and formed the core of several distinct Indian communities, such as the [Ismaili Muslim](/wiki/Ismailism "Ismailism") and [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") communities. While building the railway through [Tsavo](/wiki/Tsavo "Tsavo"), a number of the Indian railway workers and local African labourers were attacked by two lions known as the [Tsavo maneaters](/wiki/Tsavo_maneaters "Tsavo maneaters").[[68]](#cite_note-70)

At the outbreak of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") in August 1914, the governors of [British East Africa](/wiki/British_East_Africa "British East Africa") (as the protectorate was generally known) and [German East Africa](/wiki/German_East_Africa "German East Africa") initially agreed on a truce in an attempt to keep the young colonies out of direct hostilities. But Lieutenant Colonel [Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck](/wiki/Paul_von_Lettow-Vorbeck "Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck"), the German military commander, determined to tie down as many British resources as possible. Completely cut off from Germany, Lettow-Vorbeck conducted [an effective guerrilla warfare campaign](/wiki/East_African_campaign_(World_War_I) "East African campaign (World War I)"), living off the land, capturing British supplies, and remaining undefeated. He eventually surrendered in [Northern Rhodesia](/wiki/Northern_Rhodesia "Northern Rhodesia") (today Zambia) 14 days after the Armistice was signed in 1918.[[67]](#cite_note-R._Mugo_Gatheru_2005-69)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Kurve_bei_Mombasa.jpg/500px-Kurve_bei_Mombasa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kurve_bei_Mombasa.jpg)

The [Kenya–Uganda Railway](/wiki/Uganda_Railway "Uganda Railway") near [Mombasa](/wiki/Mombasa "Mombasa"), about 1899

To chase von Lettow, the British deployed the [British Indian Army](/wiki/British_Indian_Army "British Indian Army") troops from India but needed large numbers of porters to overcome the formidable logistics of transporting supplies far into the interior on foot. The [Carrier Corps](/wiki/Carrier_Corps "Carrier Corps") was formed and ultimately mobilised over 400,000 Africans, contributing to their long-term politicisation.[[67]](#cite_note-R._Mugo_Gatheru_2005-69)

In 1920, the East Africa Protectorate was turned into a colony and renamed Kenya after its highest mountain.[[66]](#cite_note-Encyclopædia_Britannica-68)

During the early part of the 20th century, the interior central highlands were settled by British and other European farmers, who became wealthy farming coffee and tea.[[69]](#cite_note-71) One depiction of this period of change from a colonist's perspective is found in the memoir *[Out of Africa](/wiki/Out_of_Africa "Out of Africa")* by Danish author Baroness Karen von Blixen-Finecke, published in 1937. By the 1930s, approximately 30,000 white settlers lived in the area and gained a political voice because of their contribution to the market economy.[[67]](#cite_note-R._Mugo_Gatheru_2005-69)

The central highlands were already home to over a million members of the Kikuyu people, most of whom had no land claims in European terms and lived as itinerant farmers. To protect their own interests the settlers banned the growing of coffee and introduced a hut tax, and the landless were granted less and less land in exchange for their labour. A massive exodus to the cities ensued as their ability to make a living from the land dwindled.[[67]](#cite_note-R._Mugo_Gatheru_2005-69) By the 1950s there were 80,000 [white settlers](/wiki/Whites_in_Kenya "Whites in Kenya") living in Kenya.[[70]](#cite_note-72)

Throughout [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), [Kenya was](/wiki/Kenya_in_World_War_II "Kenya in World War II") an important source of manpower and agriculture for the United Kingdom. Kenya itself was the site of [fighting](/wiki/East_African_Campaign_(World_War_II) "East African Campaign (World War II)") between Allied forces and Italian troops in 1940–41, when Italian forces invaded. [Wajir](/wiki/Wajir "Wajir") and [Malindi](/wiki/Malindi "Malindi") were bombed as well.

### Mau Mau Uprising

Further information: [Mau Mau Uprising](/wiki/Mau_Mau_Uprising "Mau Mau Uprising")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Statue_of_Dedan_Kimathi_Nairobi%2C_Kenya.jpg/250px-Statue_of_Dedan_Kimathi_Nairobi%2C_Kenya.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_Dedan_Kimathi_Nairobi,_Kenya.jpg)

A statue of [Dedan Kimathi](/wiki/Dedan_Kimathi "Dedan Kimathi"), a Kenyan leader with the [Mau Mau](/wiki/Kenya_Land_and_Freedom_Army "Kenya Land and Freedom Army") who fought against the British colonial system in the 1950s

From October 1952 to December 1959, Kenya was in a state of emergency arising from the [Mau Mau rebellion](/wiki/Mau_Mau_Uprising "Mau Mau Uprising") against British rule. The Mau Mau, also known as the Kenya Land and Freedom Army, were primarily [Kikuyu](/wiki/Kikuyu_people "Kikuyu people"), [Embu](/wiki/Embu_people "Embu people") and [Meru](/wiki/Meru_people "Meru people") people. During the colonial administration's crackdown, over 11,000 freedom fighters had been killed, along with 100 British troops and 2,000 Kenyan loyalist soldiers. War crimes were committed on both sides of the conflict, including the publicised [Lari massacre](/wiki/Lari_massacre "Lari massacre") and the [Hola massacre](/wiki/Hola_massacre "Hola massacre"). The governor requested and obtained British and African troops, including the [King's African Rifles](/wiki/King%27s_African_Rifles "King's African Rifles"). The British began counter-insurgency operations. In May 1953, General Sir [George Erskine](/wiki/George_Erskine "George Erskine") took charge as commander-in-chief of the colony's armed forces, with the personal backing of [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill").[[71]](#cite_note-Wunyabari_O._Maloba_1993-73)

The capture of [Waruhiu Itote](/wiki/Waruhiu_Itote "Waruhiu Itote") (nom de guerre *"General China"*) on 15 January 1954 and the subsequent interrogation led to a better understanding of the Mau Mau command structure for the British. [Operation Anvil](/wiki/Mau_Mau_Uprising#British_gain_the_initiative "Mau Mau Uprising") opened on 24 April 1954, after weeks of planning by the army with the approval of the War Council. The operation effectively placed Nairobi under military siege. Nairobi's occupants were screened, and suspected Mau Mau supporters moved to detention camps. More than 80,000 Kikuyu were [held in detention camps](/wiki/List_of_British_Detention_Camps_during_the_Mau_Mau_Uprising "List of British Detention Camps during the Mau Mau Uprising") without trial, often subject to brutal treatment.[[72]](#cite_note-74) The Home Guard formed the core of the government's strategy as it was composed of loyalist Africans, not foreign forces such as the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") and King's African Rifles.[[73]](#cite_note-75)

The capture of [Dedan Kimathi](/wiki/Dedan_Kimathi "Dedan Kimathi") on 21 October 1956 in [Nyeri](/wiki/Nyeri "Nyeri") signified the ultimate defeat of the Mau Mau and essentially ended the military offensive.[[71]](#cite_note-Wunyabari_O._Maloba_1993-73) During this period, substantial governmental changes to land tenure occurred. The most important of these was the [Swynnerton Plan](/wiki/Swynnerton_Plan "Swynnerton Plan"), which was used to both reward loyalists and punish Mau Mau. This left roughly 1/3rd of Kikuyu bereft of any tenancy land arrangement and thus propertyless at the time of independence.[[74]](#cite_note-76)

### Somalis of Kenya referendum, 1962

Further information: [Somalis in Kenya](/wiki/Somalis_in_Kenya "Somalis in Kenya")

Before Kenya got its independence, [Somali ethnic people in present-day Kenya](/wiki/Somalis_in_Kenya "Somalis in Kenya") in the areas of [Northern Frontier Districts](/wiki/Northern_Frontier_Districts "Northern Frontier Districts") petitioned Her Majesty's Government not to be included in Kenya. The colonial government decided to hold Kenya's first referendum in 1962 to check the willingness of [Somalis in Kenya](/wiki/Somalis_in_Kenya "Somalis in Kenya") to join [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia").[[75]](#cite_note-77)

The result of the referendum showed that 86% of Somalis in Kenya wished to join [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), yet the British colonial administration rejected the result and the Somalis remained in Kenya.[[76]](#cite_note-78)[[77]](#cite_note-79)

### Early independence period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Jomo_Kenyatta.jpg/250px-Jomo_Kenyatta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jomo_Kenyatta.jpg)

The first president and founding father of Kenya, [Jomo Kenyatta](/wiki/Jomo_Kenyatta "Jomo Kenyatta")

The first direct elections for native Kenyans to the Legislative Council took place in 1957.

Despite British hopes of handing power to "moderate" local rivals, it was the [Kenya African National Union](/wiki/Kenya_African_National_Union "Kenya African National Union") (KANU) of [Jomo Kenyatta](/wiki/Jomo_Kenyatta "Jomo Kenyatta") that formed a government. The Colony of Kenya and the Protectorate of Kenya each came to an end on 12 December 1963, with independence conferred on all of Kenya. The UK ceded sovereignty over the Colony of Kenya. The Sultan of Zanzibar agreed that simultaneously with the independence of the Colony he would cease to have sovereignty over the Protectorate of Kenya, so that all of Kenya would become one sovereign state.[[78]](#cite_note-Kenneth_Roberts-Wray_1966._P._762-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81) In this way, Kenya became an independent country under the Kenya Independence Act 1963 of the United Kingdom. On 12 December 1964, Kenya became a republic under the name "Republic of Kenya".[[78]](#cite_note-Kenneth_Roberts-Wray_1966._P._762-80)

Concurrently, the Kenyan army fought the [Shifta War](/wiki/Shifta_War "Shifta War") against ethnic Somali rebels inhabiting the [Northern Frontier District](/wiki/Northern_Frontier_District "Northern Frontier District") who wanted to join their kin in the [Somali Republic](/wiki/Somali_Republic "Somali Republic") to the north.[[80]](#cite_note-82) A ceasefire was eventually reached with the signing of the Arusha Memorandum in October 1967, but relative insecurity prevailed through 1969.[[81]](#cite_note-Hogg1986-83)[[82]](#cite_note-Howell1968-84) To discourage further invasions, Kenya signed a defence pact with [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia") in 1969, which is still in effect.[[83]](#cite_note-85)[[84]](#cite_note-86)

### First presidency

Further information: [Presidency of Jomo Kenyatta](/wiki/Presidency_of_Jomo_Kenyatta "Presidency of Jomo Kenyatta") and [Jomo Kenyatta](/wiki/Jomo_Kenyatta "Jomo Kenyatta")

On 12 December 1964, the Republic of Kenya was proclaimed, and [Jomo Kenyatta](/wiki/Jomo_Kenyatta "Jomo Kenyatta") became Kenya's first president.[[85]](#cite_note-87) Under Kenyatta, corruption became widespread throughout the government, civil service, and business community. Kenyatta and his family were tied up with this corruption as they enriched themselves through the mass purchase of property after 1963. Their acquisitions in the Central, Rift Valley, and Coast Provinces aroused great anger among landless Kenyans. His family used his presidential position to circumvent legal or administrative obstacles to acquiring property. The Kenyatta family also heavily invested in the coastal hotel business, with Kenyatta personally owning the Leonard Beach Hotel.[[86]](#cite_note-88)

Kenyatta's mixed legacy was highlighted at the 10-year anniversary of Kenya's independence. A December 1973 article in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* praised Kenyatta's leadership and Kenya for emerging as a model of pragmatism and conservatism. Kenya's GDP had increased at an annual rate of 6.6%, higher than the population growth rate of more than 3%.[[87]](#cite_note-89) But [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") responded to the article by stating the cost of the stability in terms of human rights abuses. The opposition party started by [Oginga Odinga](/wiki/Oginga_Odinga "Oginga Odinga")—[Kenya People's Union](/wiki/Kenya_People%27s_Union "Kenya People's Union") (KPU)—was banned in 1969 after the [Kisumu Massacre](/wiki/Kisumu_Massacre "Kisumu Massacre") and KPU leaders were still in detention without trial in gross violation of the [U.N. Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/U.N._Declaration_of_Human_Rights "U.N. Declaration of Human Rights").[[88]](#cite_note-sproul-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91) The Kenya Students Union, [Jehovah Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah_Witnesses "Jehovah Witnesses") and all opposition parties were outlawed.[[88]](#cite_note-sproul-90) Kenyatta ruled until his death on 22 August 1978.[[90]](#cite_note-92)

### Moi era

Further information: [Daniel arap Moi](/wiki/Daniel_arap_Moi "Daniel arap Moi"), [Presidency of Daniel Moi](/wiki/Presidency_of_Daniel_Moi "Presidency of Daniel Moi"), [1978 Kenyan presidential election](/wiki/1978_Kenyan_presidential_election "1978 Kenyan presidential election"), [1988 Kenyan general election](/wiki/1988_Kenyan_general_election "1988 Kenyan general election"), and [1992 Kenyan general election](/wiki/1992_Kenyan_general_election "1992 Kenyan general election")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Moi_and_Bush.jpg/250px-Moi_and_Bush.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moi_and_Bush.jpg)

[Daniel arap Moi](/wiki/Daniel_arap_Moi "Daniel arap Moi"), Kenya's second President, and [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"), 2001

After Kenyatta died, [Daniel arap Moi](/wiki/Daniel_arap_Moi "Daniel arap Moi") became president. He retained the presidency, running unopposed in elections held in 1979, 1983 ([snap elections](/wiki/Snap_election "Snap election")), and 1988, all of which were held under the single-party constitution. The 1983 elections were held a year early, and were a direct result of [a failed military coup](/wiki/1982_Kenyan_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_attempt "1982 Kenyan coup d'état attempt") on 2 August 1982.

The 1982 coup was masterminded by a low-ranking Air Force serviceman, Senior Private [Hezekiah Ochuka](/wiki/Hezekiah_Ochuka "Hezekiah Ochuka"), and was staged mainly by enlisted men of the Air Force. It was quickly suppressed by forces commanded by Chief of General Staff [Mahamoud Mohamed](/wiki/Mahamoud_Mohamed "Mahamoud Mohamed"), a veteran Somali military official.[[91]](#cite_note-93) They included the General Service Unit (GSU)—a paramilitary wing of the police—and later the regular police.

On the heels of the [Garissa Massacre](/wiki/Garissa_Massacre "Garissa Massacre") of 1980, Kenyan troops committed the [Wagalla massacre](/wiki/Wagalla_massacre "Wagalla massacre") in 1984 against thousands of civilians in [Wajir County](/wiki/Wajir_County "Wajir County"). An official probe into the atrocities was later ordered in 2011.[[92]](#cite_note-94)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

The election held in 1988 saw the advent of the *mlolongo* (queuing) system, where voters were supposed to line up behind their favoured candidates instead of casting a secret ballot.[[93]](#cite_note-95) This was seen as the climax of a very undemocratic regime and led to widespread agitation for constitutional reform. Several contentious clauses, including the one that allowed for only one political party, were changed in the following years.[[94]](#cite_note-96)

### Transition to multiparty democracy

In 1991, Kenya transitioned to a multiparty political system after 26 years of single-party rule. On 28 October 1992, Moi dissolved parliament, five months before the end of his term. As a result, preparations began for all elective seats in parliament as well as the president. The election was scheduled to take place on 7 December 1992, but delays led to its postponement to 29 December. Apart from KANU, the ruling party, other parties represented in the elections included [FORD](/wiki/Forum_for_the_Restoration_of_Democracy "Forum for the Restoration of Democracy") Kenya and FORD Asili. This election was marked by large-scale intimidation of opponents and harassment of election officials. It resulted in an economic crisis propagated by ethnic violence as the president was accused of rigging electoral results to retain power.[[95]](#cite_note-Keith-97)[[96]](#cite_note-Goldsworthy-98)[[97]](#cite_note-ParliamentKe-99) This election was a turning point for Kenya as it signified the beginning of the end of Moi's leadership and the rule of KANU. Moi retained the presidency and [George Saitoti](/wiki/George_Saitoti "George Saitoti") became vice president. Although it held on to power, KANU won 100 seats and lost 88 seats to the six opposition parties.[[95]](#cite_note-Keith-97)[[97]](#cite_note-ParliamentKe-99)

The 1992 elections marked the beginning of multiparty politics after more than 25 years of KANU rule.[[95]](#cite_note-Keith-97) Following skirmishes in the aftermath of the elections, 5,000 people were killed and another 75,000 displaced from their homes.[[98]](#cite_note-100) In the next five years, many political alliances were formed in preparation for the next elections. In 1994, [Jaramogi Oginga Odinga](/wiki/Jaramogi_Oginga_Odinga "Jaramogi Oginga Odinga") died and several coalitions joined his FORD Kenya party to form a new party, United National Democratic Alliance. This party was plagued with disagreements. In 1995, [Richard Leakey](/wiki/Richard_Leakey "Richard Leakey") formed the Safina party, but it was denied registration until November 1997.[[99]](#cite_note-BBCKenyaHist-101)

In 1996, KANU revised the constitution to allow Moi to remain president for another term. Subsequently, Moi stood for reelection and won a 5th term in 1997.[[100]](#cite_note-102) His win was strongly criticised by his major opponents, [Kibaki](/wiki/Mwai_Kibaki "Mwai Kibaki") and [Odinga](/wiki/Raila_Odinga "Raila Odinga"), as fraudulent.[[99]](#cite_note-BBCKenyaHist-101)[[101]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-103) Following this win, Moi was constitutionally barred from another presidential term. Beginning in 1998, he attempted to influence the country's succession politics to have [Uhuru Kenyatta](/wiki/Uhuru_Kenyatta "Uhuru Kenyatta") elected in the 2002 elections.[[102]](#cite_note-104)

### President Kibaki and the road to a new constitution

Further information: [Mwai Kibaki](/wiki/Mwai_Kibaki "Mwai Kibaki"), [Presidency of Mwai Kibaki](/wiki/Presidency_of_Mwai_Kibaki "Presidency of Mwai Kibaki"), [2002 Kenyan general election](/wiki/2002_Kenyan_general_election "2002 Kenyan general election"), and [2007 Kenyan general election](/wiki/2007_Kenyan_general_election "2007 Kenyan general election")

Moi's plan to be replaced by Uhuru Kenyatta failed, and [Mwai Kibaki](/wiki/Mwai_Kibaki "Mwai Kibaki"), running for the opposition coalition "National Rainbow Coalition" ([NARC](/wiki/National_Rainbow_Coalition "National Rainbow Coalition")), was elected president. David Anderson (2003) reports the elections were judged free and fair by local and international observers, and seemed to mark a turning point in Kenya's democratic evolution.[[101]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-103)

In 2005, Kenyans rejected a plan to replace the 1963 independence constitution with a new one.[[103]](#cite_note-105) As a result, the elections of 2007 took place following the procedure set by the old constitution. Kibaki was reelected in highly contested elections marred by [political and ethnic violence](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Kenyan_crisis "2007–08 Kenyan crisis"). The main opposition leader, Raila Odinga, claimed the election was rigged and that he was the rightfully elected president. In the ensuing violence, 1,500 people were killed and another 600,000 internally displaced, making it the worst post-election violence in Kenya. To stop the death and displacement of people, Kibaki and Odinga agreed to work together, with the latter taking the position of a prime minister.[[104]](#cite_note-106) This made Odinga [the second prime minister of Kenya](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Kenya "Prime Minister of Kenya").

In July 2010, Kenya partnered with other East African countries to form the new East African Common Market within the [East African Community](/wiki/East_African_Community "East African Community").[[105]](#cite_note-107) In 2011, [Kenya began sending troops](/wiki/Operation_Linda_Nchi "Operation Linda Nchi") to Somalia to fight the terror group [Al-Shabaab](/wiki/Al-Shabaab_(militant_group) "Al-Shabaab (militant group)").[[106]](#cite_note-108) In mid-2011, two consecutive missed rainy seasons precipitated the worst [drought in East Africa](/wiki/2011_East_Africa_drought "2011 East Africa drought") in 60 years. The northwestern [Turkana region](/wiki/Turkana_District "Turkana District") was especially affected,[[107]](#cite_note-RedCross-109) with local schools shut down as a result.[[108]](#cite_note-Kscafthit-110) The crisis was reportedly over by early 2012 because of coordinated relief efforts. Aid agencies subsequently shifted their emphasis to recovery initiatives, including digging irrigation canals and distributing plant seeds.[[109]](#cite_note-Unsfisiobrr-111)

In August 2010, Kenyans held a [referendum](/wiki/2010_Kenyan_constitutional_referendum "2010 Kenyan constitutional referendum") and passed [a new constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Kenya "Constitution of Kenya"), which limited presidential powers and devolved the central government.[[99]](#cite_note-BBCKenyaHist-101) Following the passage of the new constitution, Kenya became a [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") [representative democratic](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") republic, whereby the [President of Kenya](/wiki/President_of_Kenya "President of Kenya") is both [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), and of a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). The new constitution also states that [executive powers](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power") are exercised by the executive branch of government, headed by the president, who chairs a cabinet composed of people chosen from outside parliament. [Legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power") is vested exclusively in [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Kenya "Parliament of Kenya"). The [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary") is independent of the executive and the legislature.

### Kenyatta and Ruto presidencies

Main articles: [Presidency of Uhuru Kenyatta](/wiki/Presidency_of_Uhuru_Kenyatta "Presidency of Uhuru Kenyatta") and [Presidency of William Ruto](/wiki/Presidency_of_William_Ruto "Presidency of William Ruto")

[![Uhuru Kenyatta in 2014](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Uhuru_Kenyatta.png/250px-Uhuru_Kenyatta.png)](/wiki/File:Uhuru_Kenyatta.png)

[Uhuru Kenyatta](/wiki/Uhuru_Kenyatta "Uhuru Kenyatta")

In 2013, Kenya held its [first general elections](/wiki/2013_Kenyan_general_election "2013 Kenyan general election") under the 2010 constitution. [Uhuru Kenyatta](/wiki/Uhuru_Kenyatta "Uhuru Kenyatta") won, though the result was [unsuccessfully challenged](/wiki/2013_Kenyan_presidential_election_petition "2013 Kenyan presidential election petition") at the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Kenya "Supreme Court of Kenya") by opposition leader [Raila Odinga](/wiki/Raila_Odinga "Raila Odinga").[[110]](#cite_note-112) Kenyatta was re-elected in 2017 in [another disputed election](/wiki/2017_Kenyan_general_election "2017 Kenyan general election"). Odinga accused the [Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission](/wiki/Independent_Electoral_and_Boundaries_Commission "Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission") of mismanaging the election and Kenyatta and his party of rigging. The Supreme Court overturned the results in a landmark ruling that established its position as an independent check on the president.[[111]](#cite_note-113)[[112]](#cite_note-114) In the repeated election, Kenyatta won after Odinga refused to participate, citing irregularities.[[113]](#cite_note-115)[[114]](#cite_note-116)

In March 2018, a historic [handshake](/wiki/2018_Kenya_handshake "2018 Kenya handshake") between Kenyatta and Odinga signaled a period of reconciliation followed by economic growth and increased stability.[[115]](#cite_note-117)[[116]](#cite_note-118) Between 2019 and 2021, the two combined efforts to promote major changes to the Kenyan constitution, labelled the "Building Bridges Initiative" (BBI). They said their efforts were to improve inclusion and overcome the country's election system that often resulted in post-election violence.[[117]](#cite_note-bbi_blocked_2021_05_14_bbc-119)[[118]](#cite_note-kenyan_court_2021_05_13_reuters-120) The BBI proposal called for broad expansion of the legislative and executive branches, including the creation of a [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister") with two deputies and an official leader of the opposition, reverting to selecting cabinet ministers from among the elected Members of Parliament, establishing up to 70 new [constituencies](/wiki/Constituencies "Constituencies"), and adding up to 300 unelected members of Parliament (under an "[affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action "Affirmative action")" plan).[[117]](#cite_note-bbi_blocked_2021_05_14_bbc-119)[[118]](#cite_note-kenyan_court_2021_05_13_reuters-120)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/President_Ruto_Potrait_2022.jpg/250px-President_Ruto_Potrait_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Ruto_Potrait_2022.jpg)

[William Ruto](/wiki/William_Ruto "William Ruto")

Critics saw this as an attempt to reward political dynasties and blunt Deputy President [William Ruto](/wiki/William_Ruto "William Ruto")'s efforts to seek the presidency, and bloat the government at a high cost to the debt-laded country.[[117]](#cite_note-bbi_blocked_2021_05_14_bbc-119)[[118]](#cite_note-kenyan_court_2021_05_13_reuters-120) Ultimately, in May 2021, the [Kenyan High Court](/wiki/High_Court_(Kenya) "High Court (Kenya)") ruled the BBI unconstitutional, because it was an effort of the government rather than a [popular initiative](/wiki/Popular_initiative "Popular initiative").[[117]](#cite_note-bbi_blocked_2021_05_14_bbc-119)[[118]](#cite_note-kenyan_court_2021_05_13_reuters-120) The court sharply criticized Kenyatta, laying out grounds for his being sued or even [impeached](/wiki/Impeachment "Impeachment"). The ruling was seen as a major defeat for both Kenyatta and Odinga, but a boon to Odinga's future presidential-election rival, Ruto.[[117]](#cite_note-bbi_blocked_2021_05_14_bbc-119)[[118]](#cite_note-kenyan_court_2021_05_13_reuters-120) In August 2021, Kenya's [Court of Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_of_Kenya "Court of Appeal of Kenya") upheld the High Court's judgement in an appeal from the BBI Secretariat .[[119]](#cite_note-121)

Ruto narrowly won the [2022 presidential election](/wiki/2022_Kenyan_general_election "2022 Kenyan general election"), defeating Odinga to become Kenya's fifth president.[[120]](#cite_note-122)[[121]](#cite_note-123) In 2024, Ruto and the [Kenya Kwanza](/wiki/Kenya_Kwanza "Kenya Kwanza") coalition faced [popular protests](/wiki/Kenya_Protests_(2024) "Kenya Protests (2024)") over the [Kenyan Finance Bill 2024](/wiki/Kenya_Finance_Bill_2024 "Kenya Finance Bill 2024").[[122]](#cite_note-124)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Kenya](/wiki/Geography_of_Kenya "Geography of Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Un-kenya.png/250px-Un-kenya.png)](/wiki/File:Un-kenya.png)

A map of Kenya. The label "Sudan" at upper left is now incorrect; the area is now part of [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan").

At 580,367 km2 (224,081 sq mi),[[11]](#cite_note-cia-11) Kenya is the world's 47th-largest country (after [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar")). It lies between latitudes [5°N](/wiki/5th_parallel_north "5th parallel north") and [5°S](/wiki/5th_parallel_south "5th parallel south"), and longitudes [34°](/wiki/34th_meridian_east "34th meridian east") and [42°E](/wiki/42nd_meridian_east "42nd meridian east"). From the coast on the Indian Ocean, the low plains rise to central highlands which are bisected by the [Great Rift Valley](/wiki/Kenyan_Rift_Valley "Kenyan Rift Valley"), and fertile plateaus lie on either side, around [Lake Victoria](/wiki/Lake_Victoria "Lake Victoria") and to the east.[[123]](#cite_note-125)[[124]](#cite_note-126)

The Kenyan Highlands are one of the most successful agricultural production regions in Africa.[[125]](#cite_note-:0-127) The highlands are the site of the highest point in Kenya and the second highest peak on the continent: [Mount Kenya](/wiki/Mount_Kenya "Mount Kenya"), which reaches a height of 5,199 m (17,057 ft) and is the site of glaciers. [Mount Kilimanjaro](/wiki/Mount_Kilimanjaro "Mount Kilimanjaro") (5,895 m or 19,341 ft) can be seen from Kenya to the south of the Tanzanian border.

### Climate

Kenya's climate varies from tropical along the coast, to temperate inland, to [arid](/wiki/Arid "Arid") in the north and northeast parts of the country. The area receives a great deal of sunshine every month. It is usually cool at night and early in the morning inland at higher elevations.

The "long rains" season occurs from March/April to May/June. The "short rains" season occurs from October to November/December. The rainfall is sometimes heavy and often falls in the afternoons and evenings. Climate change is altering the natural pattern of the rainfall period, causing an extension of the short rains – which has led to floods –[[126]](#cite_note-128) and reducing the drought cycle from every ten years to annual events, producing strong droughts such as the [2008–09 Kenya Drought](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Kenya_Drought "2008–09 Kenya Drought").[[127]](#cite_note-129)

The temperature remains high throughout these months of tropical rain. The hottest period is February and March, leading into the season of the long rains, and the coldest is in July, until mid-August.[[128]](#cite_note-:3-130)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Koppen-Geiger_Map_KEN_present.svg/330px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_KEN_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_KEN_present.svg)

[Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map for Kenya for 1980–2016

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Koppen-Geiger_Map_KEN_future.svg/330px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_KEN_future.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_KEN_future.svg)

2071–2100 map under [the most intense climate change scenario](/wiki/Representative_Concentration_Pathway "Representative Concentration Pathway"). Mid-range scenarios are currently considered more likely.[[129]](#cite_note-Climate_change_in_Kenya_HausfatherPeters2020-131)[[130]](#cite_note-Climate_change_in_Kenya_Schuur2022-132)[[131]](#cite_note-Climate_change_in_Kenya_Phiddian2022-133)

[Climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") is posing an increasing threat to global socioeconomic development and environmental sustainability.[[132]](#cite_note-Climate_change_in_Kenya_ReliefWeb-134) Developing countries with low adaptive capacity and high vulnerability to the phenomenon are disproportionately affected. [Climate change in Kenya](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Kenya "Climate change in Kenya") is increasingly impacting the lives of Kenya's citizens and the [environment](/wiki/Natural_environment "Natural environment").[[132]](#cite_note-Climate_change_in_Kenya_ReliefWeb-134) Climate change has led to more frequent [extreme weather](/wiki/Extreme_weather "Extreme weather") events like [droughts](https://www.c2es.org/content/drought-and-climate-change/) which last longer than usual, irregular and unpredictable rainfall, flooding and increasing temperatures.

The effects of these climatic changes have made already existing challenges with [water security](/wiki/Water_security "Water security"), [food security](/wiki/Food_security "Food security") and [economic growth](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth") even more difficult. Harvests and agricultural production which account for about 33%[[133]](#cite_note-Climate_change_in_Kenya_:0-135) of total [Gross Domestic Product (GDP)](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product")[[134]](#cite_note-Climate_change_in_Kenya_UNICEF-136) are also at risk. The increased temperatures, [rainfall](/wiki/Rain "Rain") variability in [arid](/wiki/Arid "Arid") and [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") areas, and strong [winds](/wiki/Wind "Wind") associated with [tropical](/wiki/Tropical "Tropical") [cyclones](/wiki/Cyclone "Cyclone") have combined to create favourable conditions for the breeding and [migration](/wiki/Migration_(ecology) "Migration (ecology)") of pests.[[135]](#cite_note-Climate_change_in_Kenya_Salih-137) An increase in temperature of up to 2.5 °C by 2050 is predicted to increase the frequency of extreme events such as [floods](/wiki/Flood "Flood") and [droughts](/wiki/Drought "Drought").[[132]](#cite_note-Climate_change_in_Kenya_ReliefWeb-134)

### Wildlife

Main articles: [Wildlife of Kenya](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Kenya "Wildlife of Kenya") and [Environmental issues in Kenya](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_Kenya "Environmental issues in Kenya")

Kenya has extensive land area devoted to [wildlife](/wiki/Wildlife "Wildlife") habitats, including the [Tsavo game park](/wiki/Tsavo "Tsavo") and [Masai Mara](/wiki/Masai_Mara "Masai Mara"), where [blue wildebeest](/wiki/Blue_Wildebeest "Blue Wildebeest") and other [bovids](/wiki/Bovid "Bovid") participate in a large-scale [annual migration](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/migration "wikt:migration"). More than one million wildebeest and 200,000 zebras participate in the migration across the [Mara River](/wiki/Mara_River "Mara River").[[136]](#cite_note-138)

The ["Big Five"](/wiki/Big_Five_Game "Big Five Game") game animals of Africa, that is the [lion](/wiki/Lion "Lion"), [leopard](/wiki/Leopard "Leopard"), [buffalo](/wiki/African_buffalo "African buffalo"), [rhinoceros](/wiki/Rhinoceros "Rhinoceros"), and [elephant](/wiki/African_elephant "African elephant"), can be found in Kenya and in the Masai Mara in particular. A significant population of other wild animals, reptiles, and birds can be found in the [national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Kenya "List of national parks of Kenya") and game reserves in the country. The annual animal migration occurs between June and September, with millions of animals taking part, attracting valuable foreign tourism. Two million wildebeest migrate a distance of 2,900 kilometres (1,802 mi) from the [Serengeti](/wiki/Serengeti "Serengeti") in neighbouring Tanzania to the Masai Mara[[137]](#cite_note-139) in Kenya, in a constant clockwise fashion, searching for food and water supplies. This Serengeti Migration of the wildebeest is listed among the [Seven Natural Wonders of Africa](/wiki/Seven_Natural_Wonders_of_Africa "Seven Natural Wonders of Africa").[[138]](#cite_note-140)

### Conservation

Kenya had a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 4.2/10, ranking it 133rd globally out of 172 countries.[[139]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-141) Conservation International operates in Kenya through [Conservation International Kenya](/wiki/Conservation_International_Kenya "Conservation International Kenya"), a country programme founded in 2014 that reports work spanning nearly 4.9 million hectares, including forest and rangeland conservation and restoration, ecotourism, and climate-mitigation activities such as [REDD+](/wiki/REDD%2B "REDD+").[[140]](#cite_note-CIKenyaPlaces-142)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Politics of Kenya](/wiki/Politics_of_Kenya "Politics of Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/President_Ruto_Potrait_2022_%28cropped%29.jpg/120px-President_Ruto_Potrait_2022_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Ruto_Potrait_2022_(cropped).jpg)

[William Ruto](/wiki/William_Ruto "William Ruto")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Kenya "President of Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Kithure_Kindiki_in_office.jpg/250px-Kithure_Kindiki_in_office.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kithure_Kindiki_in_office.jpg)

[Kithure Kindiki](/wiki/Kithure_Kindiki "Kithure Kindiki")  
[Deputy President](/wiki/Deputy_President_of_Kenya "Deputy President of Kenya")

Kenya is a [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") [representative democratic](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") republic with a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). The president is [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"). [Executive power](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power") is exercised by the government. [Legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power") is vested in the government, the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Kenya "National Assembly of Kenya") and the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Kenya "Senate of Kenya"). The [Judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary") is independent of the executive and the legislature. There has been growing concern, especially during former president Daniel arap Moi's tenure, that the executive was increasingly meddling with the affairs of the judiciary.[[141]](#cite_note-143)

Kenya has high levels of corruption according to [Transparency International](/wiki/Transparency_International "Transparency International")'s [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index") (CPI), a metric which attempts to gauge the prevalence of [public-sector](/wiki/Public_sector "Public sector") corruption in various countries. In 2019, the nation placed 137th out of 180 countries in the index, with a score of 28 out of 100.[[142]](#cite_note-144) But there are several rather significant developments with regard to curbing corruption from the Kenyan government, for instance the establishment of a new and independent [Ethics and Anti-Corruption Commission (EACC)](/wiki/Ethics_and_Anti-Corruption_Commission "Ethics and Anti-Corruption Commission").[[143]](#cite_note-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/JUDICIARY.JPG/250px-JUDICIARY.JPG)](/wiki/File:JUDICIARY.JPG)

The [Supreme Court of Kenya](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Kenya "Supreme Court of Kenya") building

Following general elections held in 1997, the Constitution of Kenya Review Act, designed to pave the way for more comprehensive amendments to the Kenyan constitution, was passed by the national parliament.[[144]](#cite_note-146)

In December 2002, Kenya held democratic and open elections, which were judged free and fair by most international observers.[[145]](#cite_note-147) The 2002 elections marked an important turning point in Kenya's democratic evolution in that power was transferred peacefully from the [Kenya African National Union](/wiki/Kenya_African_National_Union "Kenya African National Union") (KANU), which had ruled the country since independence, to the [National Rainbow Coalition](/wiki/National_Rainbow_Coalition "National Rainbow Coalition") (NARC), a coalition of political parties.

Under the presidency of [Mwai Kibaki](/wiki/Mwai_Kibaki "Mwai Kibaki"), the new ruling coalition promised to focus its efforts on generating economic growth, combating corruption, improving education, and rewriting its constitution. A few of these promises have been met. There is free primary education.[[146]](#cite_note-148) In 2007, the government issued a statement declaring that from 2008, secondary education would be heavily subsidised, with the government footing all tuition fees.[[147]](#cite_note-149)

### 2013 elections and new government

Main articles: [Kenyan general election, 2013](/wiki/Kenyan_general_election,_2013 "Kenyan general election, 2013") and [Kenyan local elections, 2013](/wiki/Kenyan_local_elections,_2013 "Kenyan local elections, 2013")

Under the new constitution and with President Kibaki prohibited by term limits from running for a third term, Deputy Prime Minister [Uhuru Kenyatta](/wiki/Uhuru_Kenyatta "Uhuru Kenyatta") ran for office. He won with 50.51% of the vote in March 2013.

In December 2014, President Kenyatta signed a Security Laws Amendment Bill, which supporters of the law suggested was necessary to guard against armed groups. Opposition politicians, human rights groups, and nine Western countries criticised the security bill, arguing that it infringed on democratic freedoms. The governments of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), and [France](/wiki/France "France") also collectively issued a press statement cautioning about the law's potential impact. Through the Jubilee Coalition, the Bill was later passed on 19 December in the National Assembly under acrimonious circumstances.[[148]](#cite_note-150)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Kenya](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Kenya "Foreign relations of Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/President_Obama_visits_Nairobi_Kenya_July_2015_%2820848405843%29.jpg/250px-President_Obama_visits_Nairobi_Kenya_July_2015_%2820848405843%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Obama_visits_Nairobi_Kenya_July_2015_(20848405843).jpg)

President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") in Nairobi, July 2015

Relations with Somalia have historically been tense, although there has been some military co-ordination against Islamist insurgents. Kenya has good relations with the United Kingdom.[[149]](#cite_note-upenn-151) Kenya is one of the most [pro-American](/wiki/Pro-American "Pro-American") nations in Africa, and the wider world.[[150]](#cite_note-152)

With [International Criminal Court](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court "International Criminal Court") trial dates scheduled in 2013 for both President Kenyatta and Deputy President [William Ruto](/wiki/William_Ruto "William Ruto") related to the 2007 election aftermath, US president [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), who is half-Kenyan, chose not to visit the country during his [mid-2013 African trip](/wiki/United_States_presidential_visits_to_Sub-Saharan_Africa#Table_of_visits "United States presidential visits to Sub-Saharan Africa").[[151]](#cite_note-153) Later in the summer, Kenyatta visited China at the invitation of President [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") after a stop in Russia and not having visited the United States as president.[[152]](#cite_note-wp01-154) In July 2015, Obama visited Kenya, the first American president to visit the country while in office.[[153]](#cite_note-155)

The [British Army Training Unit Kenya](/wiki/British_Army_Training_Unit_Kenya "British Army Training Unit Kenya") (BATUK) is used for the training of [British](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") infantry battalions in the arid and rugged terrain of the [Great Rift Valley](/wiki/Great_Rift_Valley "Great Rift Valley").[[154]](#cite_note-156)[[155]](#cite_note-157)

### Armed forces

Main article: [Kenya Defence Forces](/wiki/Kenya_Defence_Forces "Kenya Defence Forces")

[![Emblem of the Kenya Defence Forces](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Emblem_of_the_Kenya_Defence_Forces.svg/250px-Emblem_of_the_Kenya_Defence_Forces.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Emblem_of_the_Kenya_Defence_Forces.svg)

Emblem of the Kenya Defence Forces

The Kenya Defence Forces are the [armed forces](/wiki/Armed_forces "Armed forces") of Kenya. The [Kenya Army](/wiki/Kenya_Army "Kenya Army"), [Kenya Navy](/wiki/Kenya_Navy "Kenya Navy"), and [Kenya Air Force](/wiki/Kenya_Air_Force "Kenya Air Force") compose the National Defence Forces. The current Kenya Defence Forces were established, and its composition laid out, in Article 241 of the 2010 [Constitution of Kenya](/wiki/Constitution_of_Kenya "Constitution of Kenya"); the KDF is governed by the Kenya Defence Forces Act of 2012.[[156]](#cite_note-158) The [President of Kenya](/wiki/President_of_Kenya "President of Kenya") is the [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of all the armed forces.

The armed forces are regularly deployed in peacekeeping missions around the world. Further, in the aftermath of the national elections of December 2007 and the violence that subsequently engulfed the country, a commission of inquiry, the [Waki Commission](/wiki/Waki_Commission "Waki Commission"), commended its readiness and adjudged it to "have performed its duty well."[[157]](#cite_note-159) Nevertheless, there have been serious allegations of human rights violations, most recently while conducting counter-insurgency operations in the [Mt Elgon](/wiki/Mt_Elgon "Mt Elgon") area[[158]](#cite_note-160) and also in the district of [Mandera](/wiki/Mandera "Mandera") central.[[159]](#cite_note-161)

Kenya's armed forces, like many government institutions in the country, have been tainted by [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Kenya "Corruption in Kenya") allegations. Because the operations of the armed forces have been traditionally cloaked by the ubiquitous blanket of "state security", the corruption has been hidden from public view, and thus less subject to public scrutiny and notoriety. This has changed recently. In what are by Kenyan standards unprecedented revelations, in 2010, credible claims of corruption were made with regard to recruitment[[160]](#cite_note-162) and procurement of armoured personnel carriers.[[161]](#cite_note-163) Further, the wisdom and prudence of certain decisions of procurement have been publicly questioned.[[162]](#cite_note-164)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Counties of Kenya](/wiki/Counties_of_Kenya "Counties of Kenya") and [Divisions of Kenya](/wiki/Divisions_of_Kenya "Divisions of Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Map_showing_counties_under_the_new_Kenyan_constitution.gif/250px-Map_showing_counties_under_the_new_Kenyan_constitution.gif)](/wiki/File:Map_showing_counties_under_the_new_Kenyan_constitution.gif)

Kenya's 47 counties

Kenya is divided into [47 semi-autonomous counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Kenya "Counties of Kenya") that are headed by governors. These 47 counties form the first-order divisions of Kenya.

The smallest administrative units in Kenya are called [locations](/wiki/Locations_of_Kenya "Locations of Kenya"). Locations often coincide with electoral wards. Locations are usually named after their central villages/towns. Many larger towns consist of several locations. Each location has a chief, appointed by the state.

Constituencies are an electoral subdivision, with each county comprising a whole number of constituencies. An interim boundaries commission was formed in 2010 to review the constituencies and in its report, it recommended the creation of an additional 80 constituencies. Previous to the 2013 elections, there were 210 [constituencies in Kenya](/wiki/Constituencies_of_Kenya "Constituencies of Kenya").[[163]](#cite_note-165)

### Human rights

See also: [Human rights in Kenya](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Kenya "Human rights in Kenya"), [LGBT rights in Kenya](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Kenya "LGBT rights in Kenya"), and [Human trafficking in Kenya](/wiki/Human_trafficking_in_Kenya "Human trafficking in Kenya")

[Homosexual acts](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") are illegal in Kenya and typically punishable by up to 14 years in prison.[[164]](#cite_note-166) According to a 2020 survey by the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center"), 83% of Kenyans believe that homosexuality should not be accepted by society.[[165]](#cite_note-pewresearch.org-167) While addressing a joint press conference together with President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") in 2015, President Kenyatta declined to assure Kenya's commitment to gay rights, saying that "the issue of gay rights is really a non-issue... But there are some things that we must admit we don't share. Our culture, our societies don't accept."[[166]](#cite_note-168)

In November 2008, [WikiLeaks](/wiki/WikiLeaks "WikiLeaks") brought wide international attention to *The Cry of Blood* report, which documents the extrajudicial killing of gangsters by the Kenyan police. In the report, the [Kenya National Commission on Human Rights](/wiki/Kenya_National_Commission_on_Human_Rights "Kenya National Commission on Human Rights") (KNCHR) reported these in their key finding "e)", stating that the [forced disappearances](/wiki/Forced_disappearance "Forced disappearance") and [extrajudicial killings](/wiki/Extrajudicial_killing "Extrajudicial killing") appeared to be official policy sanctioned by the political leadership and the police.[[167]](#cite_note-169)[[168]](#cite_note-170)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Kenya](/wiki/Economy_of_Kenya "Economy of Kenya")

Kenya's [macroeconomic](/wiki/Macroeconomics "Macroeconomics") outlook has steadily posted robust growth over the past few decades mostly from [road](/wiki/Road "Road"), [rail](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport"), [air](/wiki/Low-cost_carrier "Low-cost carrier") and [water transport](/wiki/Water_transport "Water transport") infrastructure projects as well as massive investments in [Information and Communication Technology](/wiki/Information_and_communications_technology "Information and communications technology"). The Kenyan economy is the largest in East Africa. After independence, Kenya promoted rapid economic growth through public investment, encouraged smallholder agricultural production and provided incentives for private industrial investment. Kenya is East Africa's regional transportation and financial hub. Kenya's financial sector is vibrant, well developed and diversified boasting the highest financial inclusion in the region and globally.[[169]](#cite_note-171)

Foreign investments in Kenya remain relatively weak considering the size of its economy and its level of development. As of 2022, Kenya's total FDI stock stood at US$10.4 billion, accounting for a mere 9.5% of the country's GDP.[[170]](#cite_note-172)

Kenya has a [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (HDI) of 0.628 (medium), ranked 143 out of 193 in the world as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kenya&action=edit). As of 2022, 25.4% of Kenyans lived on less than $2.15 a day.[[171]](#cite_note-mpi2024-173) Based on the most recent data from 2022, 25.4% of the population is affected by [multidimensional poverty](/wiki/Multidimensional_Poverty_Index "Multidimensional Poverty Index") and an additional 26.4% vulnerable to it.[[172]](#cite_note-mpi2023-174)[[171]](#cite_note-mpi2024-173) In 2017, Kenya ranked 92nd in the World Bank [ease of doing business](/wiki/Ease_of_doing_business_index "Ease of doing business index") rising from 113rd in 2016 (of 190 countries).[[173]](#cite_note-175) The important agricultural sector employs around 30% of the workforce.[[174]](#cite_note-176) Kenya is usually classified as a [frontier market](/wiki/Frontier_market "Frontier market") or occasionally an [emerging market](/wiki/Emerging_market "Emerging market"), and is one of the [Developing countries](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country").

The economy has seen much expansion, seen by strong performance in tourism, higher education and [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_Kenya "Telecommunications in Kenya") and decent post-drought results in agriculture, especially the vital tea sector.[[175]](#cite_note-lcweb2.loc.gov-177) Kenya's economy grew by more than 7% in 2007, and its foreign debt was greatly reduced.[[175]](#cite_note-lcweb2.loc.gov-177) This changed immediately after the disputed presidential election of December 2007, following the chaos which engulfed the country.

Telecommunications and financial activity over the last decade now comprise 62% of GDP. 22% of GDP still comes from the unreliable agricultural sector which employs 75% of the labour force (a characteristic of under-developed economies that have not attained [food security](/wiki/Food_security "Food security")). A small portion of the population relies on food aid.[[176]](#cite_note-:1-178) Industry and manufacturing is the smallest sector, accounting for 16% of GDP. The services, industry and manufacturing sectors only employ 25% of the labour force but contribute 75% of GDP.[[175]](#cite_note-lcweb2.loc.gov-177)
Kenya also exports textiles worth over $400 million under [AGOA](/wiki/AGOA "AGOA").

Privatisation of state corporations like the defunct Kenya Post and Telecommunications Company, which resulted in East Africa's most profitable company—[Safaricom](/wiki/Safaricom "Safaricom"), has led to their revival because of massive private investment.
