As of May 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kenya&action=edit), economic prospects were positive with 4–5% GDP growth expected, largely because of expansions in [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications"), transport, construction, and a recovery in [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"). The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") estimated growth of 4.3% in 2012.[[177]](#cite_note-WorldBanki-179)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Kenya_Human_Development_Index_computed_by_the_UN.png/500px-Kenya_Human_Development_Index_computed_by_the_UN.png)](/wiki/File:Kenya_Human_Development_Index_computed_by_the_UN.png)

Kenya, trends in the Human Development Index 1970–2010

In March 1996 the presidents of Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda re-established the [East African Community](/wiki/East_African_Community "East African Community") (EAC). The EAC's objectives include harmonising tariffs and customs regimes, free movement of people and improving regional infrastructures. In March 2004, the three East African countries signed a [Customs Union Agreement](/wiki/Customs_union "Customs union").

Kenya has a more developed financial services sector than its neighbours. The [Nairobi Securities Exchange](/wiki/Nairobi_Securities_Exchange "Nairobi Securities Exchange") (NSE) is ranked 4th in Africa in terms of market capitalisation. The Kenyan banking system is supervised by the [Central Bank of Kenya](/wiki/Central_Bank_of_Kenya "Central Bank of Kenya") (CBK). As of late July 2004, the system consisted of 43 commercial banks (down from 48 in 2001) and several [non-bank financial institutions](/wiki/Non-bank_financial_institution "Non-bank financial institution") including mortgage companies, four savings and loan associations and several core foreign-exchange bureaus.[[175]](#cite_note-lcweb2.loc.gov-177)

The inaugural Kenya Innovation Week (KIW) was started in 2021, from December 6 to 10, 2021, at the Kenya School of Government in Lower Kabete, Nairobi.[[178]](#cite_note-180)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Kenya](/wiki/Tourism_in_Kenya "Tourism in Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Elephants_at_Amboseli_national_park_against_Mount_Kilimanjaro.jpg/250px-Elephants_at_Amboseli_national_park_against_Mount_Kilimanjaro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elephants_at_Amboseli_national_park_against_Mount_Kilimanjaro.jpg)

[Amboseli National Park](/wiki/Amboseli_National_Park "Amboseli National Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Tsavo_east_panorama.jpg/250px-Tsavo_east_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tsavo_east_panorama.jpg)

[Tsavo East National Park](/wiki/Tsavo_East_National_Park "Tsavo East National Park")

Tourism contributes around 6% to Kenya's economy.[[179]](#cite_note-181)[Tourism in Kenya](/wiki/Tourism_in_Kenya "Tourism in Kenya") is the third-largest source of foreign exchange revenue, behind diaspora remittances and agriculture.[[180]](#cite_note-182) The Kenya Tourism Board is responsible for maintaining information pertaining to tourism in Kenya.[[181]](#cite_note-183)[[182]](#cite_note-184) The main tourist attractions are photo safaris through the [60 national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Kenya "List of national parks of Kenya") and game reserves. Game reserves include [Maasai Mara Game Reserve](/wiki/Maasai_Mara_Game_Reserve "Maasai Mara Game Reserve"), [Samburu National Reserve](/wiki/Samburu_National_Reserve "Samburu National Reserve"), [Solio Game Reserve](/wiki/Solio_Game_Reserve "Solio Game Reserve"), [Shaba National Reserve](/wiki/Shaba_National_Reserve "Shaba National Reserve"), [Buffalo Springs National Reserve](/wiki/Buffalo_Springs_National_Reserve "Buffalo Springs National Reserve") among others. Other attractions include the [wildebeest](/wiki/Wildebeest "Wildebeest") migration at the [Masaai Mara](/wiki/Masaai_Mara "Masaai Mara"); historical mosques, and colonial-era forts at [Mombasa](/wiki/Mombasa "Mombasa"), [Malindi](/wiki/Malindi "Malindi"), and [Lamu](/wiki/Lamu "Lamu"); renowned scenery such as the white-capped [Mount Kenya](/wiki/Mount_Kenya "Mount Kenya") and the [Great Rift Valley](/wiki/Great_Rift_Valley,_Kenya "Great Rift Valley, Kenya"); tea plantations at [Kericho](/wiki/Kericho "Kericho"); coffee plantations at [Thika](/wiki/Thika "Thika"); a splendid view of [Mount Kilimanjaro](/wiki/Mount_Kilimanjaro "Mount Kilimanjaro") across the border into Tanzania; and the beaches along the [Swahili Coast](/wiki/Swahili_Coast "Swahili Coast"), in the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"). Tourists, the largest number being from [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), are attracted mainly to the coastal beaches and the [game reserves](/wiki/Game_reserve "Game reserve"), notably, the expansive [East](/wiki/Tsavo_East_National_Park "Tsavo East National Park") and [Tsavo West National Park](/wiki/Tsavo_West_National_Park "Tsavo West National Park"), 20,808 square kilometres (8,034 sq mi) to the southeast.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Agriculture

Main article: [Agriculture in Kenya](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Kenya "Agriculture in Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Kenya-Tealand-Near-Kericho-2012.JPG/250px-Kenya-Tealand-Near-Kericho-2012.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kenya-Tealand-Near-Kericho-2012.JPG)

Tea farm near [Kericho](/wiki/Kericho "Kericho"), [Kericho County](/wiki/Kericho_County "Kericho County")

Agriculture is the second-largest contributor to Kenya's gross domestic product (GDP) after the service sector. In 2005, agriculture, including [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry") and fishing, accounted for 24% of GDP, as well as for 18% of wage employment and 50% of revenue from exports. The principal [cash crops](/wiki/Cash_crop "Cash crop") are tea, horticultural produce, and coffee. Horticultural produce and tea are the main growth sectors and the two most valuable of all of Kenya's exports. The production of major food staples such as [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize") is subject to sharp weather-related fluctuations. Production downturns periodically necessitate food aid—for example in 2004, due to one of Kenya's intermittent [droughts](/wiki/Droughts "Droughts").[[183]](#cite_note-185)

A consortium led by the [International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics](/wiki/International_Crops_Research_Institute_for_the_Semi-Arid_Tropics "International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics") ([ICRISAT](/wiki/ICRISAT "ICRISAT")) has had some success in helping farmers grow new [pigeon pea](/wiki/Pigeon_pea "Pigeon pea") varieties instead of maize, in particularly dry areas. Pigeon peas are very drought-resistant, so can be grown in areas with less than 650 millimetres (26 in) annual rainfall. Successive projects encouraged the commercialisation of legumes by stimulating the growth of local seed production and agro-dealer networks for distribution and marketing. This work, which included linking producers to wholesalers, helped to increase local producer prices by 20–25% in Nairobi and Mombasa. The commercialisation of the pigeon pea is now enabling some farmers to buy assets ranging from mobile phones to productive land and livestock, and is opening pathways for them to move out of poverty.[[184]](#cite_note-186)

Tea, coffee, sisal, pyrethrum, corn, and wheat are grown in the fertile highlands, one of the most successful agricultural production regions in Africa.[[125]](#cite_note-:0-127) Livestock predominates in the semi-arid savanna to the north and east. [Coconuts](/wiki/Coconuts "Coconuts"), [pineapples](/wiki/Pineapples "Pineapples"), [cashew nuts](/wiki/Cashew_nuts "Cashew nuts"), cotton, [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane"), [sisal](/wiki/Sisal "Sisal"), and corn are grown in the lower-lying areas. Kenya has not attained the level of investment and efficiency in agriculture that can guarantee food security, and coupled with resulting poverty (53% of the population lives below the poverty line), a significant portion of the population regularly starves and is heavily dependent on food aid.[[176]](#cite_note-:1-178) Poor roads, an inadequate railway network, under-used water transport, and expensive air transport have isolated mostly [arid](/wiki/Arid "Arid") and [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid "Semi-arid") areas, and farmers in other regions often leave food to rot in the fields because they cannot access markets. This was last seen in August and September 2011, prompting the [Kenyans for Kenya](/wiki/Kenyans_for_Kenya "Kenyans for Kenya") initiative by the [Red Cross](/wiki/Red_Cross "Red Cross").[[185]](#cite_note-187)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Kapsowar1.JPG/250px-Kapsowar1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kapsowar1.JPG)

Agricultural countryside in Kenya

Kenya's [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation") sector is categorised into three organizational types: [smallholder](/wiki/Smallholding "Smallholding") schemes, centrally-managed public schemes, and private/commercial irrigation schemes.

The smallholder schemes are owned, developed, and managed by individuals or groups of farmers operating as water users or self-help groups. Irrigation is carried out on individual or on group farms averaging 0.1–0.4 ha. There are about 3,000 smallholder irrigation schemes covering a total area of 47,000 ha.
The country has seven large, centrally managed irrigation schemes, namely Mwea, [Bura](/wiki/Bura_irrigation_and_Settlement_Project_(Kenya) "Bura irrigation and Settlement Project (Kenya)"), [Hola](/wiki/Hola,_Kenya "Hola, Kenya"), [Perkera](/wiki/Perkerra_River "Perkerra River"), West Kano, Bunyala, and [Ahero](/wiki/Ahero "Ahero"), covering a total area of 18,200 ha and averaging 2,600 ha per scheme. These schemes are managed by the National Irrigation Board and account for 18% of irrigated land area in Kenya.
Large-scale private commercial farms cover 45,000 hectares, accounting for 40% of irrigated land. They utilise high technology and produce high-value crops for the export market, especially flowers and vegetables.[[186]](#cite_note-188)

Kenya is the world's 3rd largest exporter of [cut flowers](/wiki/Cut_flowers "Cut flowers").[[187]](#cite_note-kenya-flower-industry-189) Roughly half of Kenya's 127 flower farms are concentrated around [Lake Naivasha](/wiki/Lake_Naivasha "Lake Naivasha"), 90 kilometres northwest of Nairobi.[[187]](#cite_note-kenya-flower-industry-189) To speed their export, Nairobi airport has a terminal dedicated to the transport of flowers and vegetables.[[187]](#cite_note-kenya-flower-industry-189)

### Industry and manufacturing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Orrling_of_Nairobi.jpg/330px-Orrling_of_Nairobi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Orrling_of_Nairobi.jpg)

The [Kenya Commercial Bank](/wiki/Kenya_Commercial_Bank "Kenya Commercial Bank") office at KENCOM House (right) in Nairobi

Although Kenya is a low middle-income country, manufacturing accounts for 14% of the GDP, with industrial activity concentrated around the three largest urban centres of [Nairobi](/wiki/Nairobi "Nairobi"), [Mombasa](/wiki/Mombasa "Mombasa"), and [Kisumu](/wiki/Kisumu "Kisumu"), and is dominated by food-processing industries such as grain milling, beer production, sugarcane crushing, and the fabrication of consumer goods, e.g., vehicles from kits.

Kenya also has a cement production industry.[[188]](#cite_note-190) Kenya has an [oil refinery](/wiki/Oil_refinery "Oil refinery") that processes imported crude petroleum into petroleum products, mainly for the domestic market. In addition, a substantial and expanding [informal sector](/wiki/Informal_sector "Informal sector") commonly referred to as *jua kali* engages in small-scale manufacturing of household goods, auto parts, and farm implements.[[189]](#cite_note-191)[[190]](#cite_note-192)

Kenya's inclusion among the beneficiaries of the US Government's African Growth and Opportunity Act ([AGOA](/wiki/AGOA "AGOA")) has given a boost to manufacturing in recent years. Since AGOA took effect in 2000, Kenya's clothing sales to the United States increased from US$44 million to US$270 million (2006).[[191]](#cite_note-193) Other initiatives to strengthen manufacturing have been the new government's favourable tax measures, including the removal of duty on capital equipment and other raw materials.[[192]](#cite_note-194)

In 2023, Kenya is in the process of constructing five industrial parks that will operate tax-free, with an anticipated completion date set for 2030. There are intentions to develop an additional 20 industrial parks in the future.[[193]](#cite_note-195)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Kenya](/wiki/Transport_in_Kenya "Transport in Kenya")

Two trans-African automobile routes pass through Kenya: the [Cairo-Cape Town Highway](/wiki/Cairo-Cape_Town_Highway "Cairo-Cape Town Highway") and the [Lagos-Mombasa Highway](/wiki/Lagos-Mombasa_Highway "Lagos-Mombasa Highway"), so the country has an extensive road network of paved and unpaved roads. Kenya's railway system links the nation's ports and major cities, connecting it with neighbouring Uganda. There are 15 airports which have paved runways.

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Kenya](/wiki/Energy_in_Kenya "Energy in Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Worker_in_Olkaria_Kenya.jpg/250px-Worker_in_Olkaria_Kenya.jpg)](/wiki/File:Worker_in_Olkaria_Kenya.jpg)

Workers at [Olkaria Geothermal Power Plant](/wiki/Olkaria_II_Geothermal_Power_Station "Olkaria II Geothermal Power Station")

The largest share of Kenya's electricity supply comes from geothermal energy,[[194]](#cite_note-196) followed by hydroelectric stations at dams along the upper [Tana River](/wiki/Tana_River_(Kenya) "Tana River (Kenya)"), as well as the Turkwel Gorge Dam in the west. A petroleum-fired plant on the coast, [geothermal facilities](/wiki/Geothermal_power_in_Kenya "Geothermal power in Kenya") at [Olkaria](/wiki/Olkaria "Olkaria") (near Nairobi), and electricity imported from [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda") make up the rest of the supply. A [2,000 MW powerline from Ethiopia](/wiki/Sodo%E2%80%93Moyale%E2%80%93Suswa_High_Voltage_Power_Line "Sodo–Moyale–Suswa High Voltage Power Line") was completed in 2022.

Kenya's installed capacity increased from 1,142 [megawatts](/wiki/Megawatts "Megawatts") between 2001 and 2003 to 2,341 in 2016.[[195]](#cite_note-197) The state-owned [Kenya Electricity Generating Company (KenGen)](/wiki/Kenya_Electricity_Generating_Company "Kenya Electricity Generating Company"), established in 1997 under the name of Kenya Power Company, handles the generation of electricity, [Kenya Electricity Transmission Company](/wiki/Kenya_Electricity_Transmission_Company "Kenya Electricity Transmission Company") handles long distance distribution of electricity, while [Kenya Power and Lighting](/wiki/Kenya_Power_and_Lighting "Kenya Power and Lighting") handles the electricity transmission and distribution system in the country. Shortfalls of electricity occur periodically, when drought reduces water flow. To become energy sufficient, Kenya has installed [wind power](/wiki/Wind_power_in_Kenya "Wind power in Kenya") and [solar power](/wiki/Solar_power_in_Kenya "Solar power in Kenya") (over 300 MW each), and aims to build a nuclear power plant by 2027.[[196]](#cite_note-198)[[197]](#cite_note-199)

Kenya has proven deposits of oil in [Turkana](/wiki/Turkana_County "Turkana County"). [Tullow Oil](/wiki/Tullow_Oil "Tullow Oil") estimates the country's oil reserves to be around one billion barrels.[[198]](#cite_note-200) Exploration is still continuing to determine whether there are more reserves. Kenya currently imports all crude petroleum requirements. Since 2013 the government resorted to importing processed white petroleum. [Kenya Pipeline Company](/wiki/Kenya_Pipeline_Company "Kenya Pipeline Company") is the state-owned parastatal mandated with the storage and transportation of white petroleum. The company was recently privatized by the government with the government owning a minor stake. Kenya has no strategic reserves and relies solely on oil marketers' 21-day oil reserves required under industry regulations. Petroleum accounts for 20%-to-25% of the national import bill.[[199]](#cite_note-201)

### Chinese investment and trade

Published comments on Kenya's [Capital FM](/wiki/98.4_Capital_FM "98.4 Capital FM") website by Liu Guangyuan, [China's ambassador to Kenya](/wiki/Chinese_Ambassador_to_Kenya "Chinese Ambassador to Kenya"), at the time of President Kenyatta's 2013 trip to Beijing, said, "Chinese investment in Kenya ... reached $474 million, representing Kenya's largest source of foreign direct investment, and ... bilateral trade ... reached $2.84 billion" in 2012. Kenyatta was "[a]ccompanied by 60 Kenyan business people [and hoped to] ... gain support from China for a planned $2.5 billion railway from the southern Kenyan port of Mombasa to neighbouring Uganda, as well as a nearly $1.8 billion dam", according to a statement from the president's office, also at the time of the trip.[[152]](#cite_note-wp01-154)

Base Titanium, a subsidiary of Base resources of Australia, shipped its first major consignment of minerals to China. About 25,000 tonnes of [ilmenite](/wiki/Ilmenite "Ilmenite") was flagged off the Kenyan coastal town of Kilifi. The first shipment was expected to earn Kenya about KSh.15–20 billion/= in earnings.[[200]](#cite_note-202) In 2014, the Chinese contracted railway project from Nairobi to Mombasa was suspended due to a dispute over compensation for land acquisition.[[201]](#cite_note-203)

### Vision 2030

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Vision2030_logo.svg/250px-Vision2030_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Vision2030_logo.svg)

The official logo of [Vision 2030](/wiki/Kenya_Vision_2030 "Kenya Vision 2030")

In 2007, the Kenyan government unveiled [Vision 2030](/wiki/Kenya_Vision_2030 "Kenya Vision 2030"), an economic development programme it hopes will put the country in the same league as the [Asian Economic Tigers](/wiki/Four_Asian_Tigers "Four Asian Tigers") by 2030. In 2013, it launched a National Climate Change Action Plan, having acknowledged that omitting climate as a key development issue in Vision 2030 was an oversight failure. The 200-page Action Plan, developed with support from the [Climate & Development Knowledge Network](/wiki/Climate_%26_Development_Knowledge_Network "Climate & Development Knowledge Network"), sets out the Government of Kenya's vision for a 'low-carbon climate resilient development pathway'. At the launch in March 2013, the Secretary of the Ministry of Planning, National Development, and Vision 2030 emphasized that climate would be a central issue in the renewed Medium-Term Plan that would be launched in the coming months. This would create a direct and robust delivery framework for the Action Plan and ensure climate change is treated as an economy-wide issue.[[202]](#cite_note-204) Furthermore, Kenya submitted an updated, more ambitious NDC on 24 December 2020, with a commitment to abate greenhouse gases by 32 percent by 2030 relative to the business-as-usual scenario and in line with its sustainable development agenda and national circumstances.[[203]](#cite_note-205)

### Oil exploration

See also: [Oil in Kenya](/wiki/Energy_in_Kenya#Petroleum "Energy in Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Kenya_Aerial_2009-08-27_14-26-44.JPG/250px-Kenya_Aerial_2009-08-27_14-26-44.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kenya_Aerial_2009-08-27_14-26-44.JPG)

[Lake Turkana](/wiki/Lake_Turkana "Lake Turkana") borders Turkana County.

Kenya has proven oil deposits in [Turkana County](/wiki/Turkana_County "Turkana County"). President Mwai Kibaki announced on 26 March 2012 that [Tullow Oil](/wiki/Tullow_Oil "Tullow Oil"), an Anglo-Irish [oil exploration](/wiki/Oil_exploration "Oil exploration") firm, had struck oil, but its commercial viability and subsequent production would take about three years to confirm.[[204]](#cite_note-206)

Early in 2006, Chinese president [Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao") signed an oil exploration contract with Kenya, part of a series of deals designed to keep Africa's natural resources flowing to China's rapidly expanding economy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Lions_Family_Portrait_Masai_Mara.jpg/250px-Lions_Family_Portrait_Masai_Mara.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lions_Family_Portrait_Masai_Mara.jpg)

A family of lions in [Maasai Mara](/wiki/Maasai_Mara "Maasai Mara")

The deal allowed for China's state-controlled offshore oil and gas company, [CNOOC](/wiki/China_National_Offshore_Oil_Corporation "China National Offshore Oil Corporation"), to prospect for oil in Kenya, which is just beginning to drill its first exploratory wells on the borders of Sudan and the disputed area of [North Eastern Province](/wiki/North_Eastern_Province,_Kenya "North Eastern Province, Kenya"), on the border with [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia") and in coastal waters. There are formal estimates of the possible reserves of oil discovered.[[205]](#cite_note-207)

### Action against pollution

In 2017, Kenya banned single-use plastic bags. According to the national environmental authority, 80% of the public has adhered to this ban. Subsequently, in 2020, the prohibition of single-use plastics was extended to protected areas, including parks and forests.[[206]](#cite_note-:4-208)

A law passed in July 2023 mandates companies to actively reduce the pollution and environmental impact caused by the products they introduce into the Kenyan market, either individually or through collective schemes. Unlike previous practices, businesses are now obligated to participate in waste collection and recycling initiatives, such as the Petco initiative established by the government in 2018.[[206]](#cite_note-:4-208)

### Labour and human capital

Kenya has a labour force of around 24 million and a total labour force participation rate of 74%, the unemployment rate in 2022 was estimated at 5.6%[[207]](#cite_note-209)

According to the World Bank's 2019 Human Capital Index (HCI), which measured human capital of the next generation, Kenya ranked first in sub-Saharan Africa with an HCI score of 0.52. The index combined several key indicators, which are school enrolment, child survival, quality of learning, healthy growth and adult survival into a single index ranging between 0–1.[[208]](#cite_note-210)

### Microfinance

Main article: [Microfinance in Kenya](/wiki/Microfinance_in_Kenya "Microfinance in Kenya")

More than 20 institutions offer business loans on a large scale, specific agriculture loans, education loans, and loans for other purposes. Additionally, there are:

* emergency loans, which are more expensive in respect to interest rates, but are quickly available
* group loans for smaller groups (four to five members) and larger groups (up to 30 members)
* women's loans, which are also available to groups of women

Out of approximately 40 million Kenyans, about 14 million are unable to receive financial service through formal loan application services, and an additional 12 million have no access to financial service institutions at all. Further, one million Kenyans are reliant on informal groups for receiving financial aid.[[209]](#cite_note-211)

To mitigate this problem, the mobile banking service [M-Pesa](/wiki/M-Pesa "M-Pesa") was launched in 2007 by [Vodafone](/wiki/Vodafone "Vodafone") and [Safaricom](/wiki/Safaricom "Safaricom"), in collaboration from the Financial Deepening Challenge Fund competition established by the UK government's Department for International Development. M-Pesa allows users to deposit, withdraw, transfer money, pay for goods and services (Lipa na M-Pesa), access credit and savings, all with a mobile device,[[210]](#cite_note-212) has provided access to digital transactions to millions of Kenyans in poverty situation.[[211]](#cite_note-213)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Kenya](/wiki/Demographics_of_Kenya "Demographics of Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Kikuyu_woman_traditional_dress.jpg/250px-Kikuyu_woman_traditional_dress.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kikuyu_woman_traditional_dress.jpg)

A [Bantu](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") [Kikuyu](/wiki/Kikuyu_people "Kikuyu people") woman in traditional attire

The census carried out by the [Kenya National Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Kenya_National_Bureau_of_Statistics "Kenya National Bureau of Statistics") in 2019 reported Kenya's population as 47,564,296.[[7]](#cite_note-KNBS2019-7) The country has a young population, with 73% of residents under 30 because of rapid [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth"),[[212]](#cite_note-214)[[213]](#cite_note-215) from 2.9 million to 40 million inhabitants over the last century.[[214]](#cite_note-216)

Nairobi is home to [Kibera](/wiki/Kibera "Kibera"), one of the world's largest slums. The shantytown is believed to house between 170,000[[215]](#cite_note-nation1-217) and one million people.[[216]](#cite_note-218) The UNHCR base in [Dadaab](/wiki/Dadaab "Dadaab") in the north houses around 500,000.[[217]](#cite_note-DadaabPop-219)

### Ethnic groups

Kenya has a diverse population that includes many of Africa's major ethnoracial and linguistic groups. Although there is no official list of Kenyan ethnic groups, the number of ethnic categories and sub-categories recorded in the country's census has changed significantly over time, expanding from 42 in 1969 to more than 120 in 2019.[[218]](#cite_note-Balaton-Chrimes2020-220) Most residents are [Bantus](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") (60%) or [Nilotes](/wiki/Nilotic_peoples "Nilotic peoples") (30%).[[219]](#cite_note-Asongumarr-221) [Cushitic](/wiki/Cushitic_languages "Cushitic languages") groups also form a small ethnic minority, as do Arabs, Indians, and Europeans.[[219]](#cite_note-Asongumarr-221)[[220]](#cite_note-Okothndaloh-222)

According to the Kenya National Bureau of Statistics (KNBS), the largest native ethnic groups in 2019 were the [Kikuyu](/wiki/Kikuyu_people "Kikuyu people") (8,148,668), [Luhya](/wiki/Luhya_people "Luhya people") (6,823,842), [Kalenjin](/wiki/Kalenjin_people "Kalenjin people") (6,358,113), [Luo](/wiki/Luo_people "Luo people") (5,066,966), [Kamba](/wiki/Kamba_people "Kamba people") (4,663,910), [Somali](/wiki/Somalis "Somalis") (2,780,502), [Kisii](/wiki/Kisii_people "Kisii people") (2,703,235), [Mijikenda](/wiki/Mijikenda_peoples "Mijikenda peoples") (2,488,691), [Meru](/wiki/Meru_people "Meru people") (1,975,869), [Maasai](/wiki/Maasai_people "Maasai people") (1,189,522), and [Turkana](/wiki/Turkana_people "Turkana people") (1,016,174). The [North Eastern Province](/wiki/North_Eastern_Province,_Kenya "North Eastern Province, Kenya") of Kenya, formerly known as NFD, is predominantly inhabited by ethnic [Somalis](/wiki/Somalis "Somalis"). Foreign-rooted populations include [Arabs](/wiki/Omanis "Omanis"), [Asians](/wiki/Indians_in_Kenya "Indians in Kenya"), and [Europeans](/wiki/White_people_in_Kenya "White people in Kenya").[[2]](#cite_note-Census2019a-2)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Kenya](/wiki/Languages_of_Kenya "Languages of Kenya")

Kenya's ethnic groups typically speak their mother tongues within their own communities. The two [official languages](/wiki/Official_language "Official language"), English and [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language"), are used in varying degrees of fluency for communication with other populations. English is widely spoken in commerce, schooling, and government.[[221]](#cite_note-Cugrwed-223) [Peri-urban](/wiki/Peri-urbanisation "Peri-urbanisation") and rural dwellers are less multilingual, with many in rural areas speaking only their native languages.[[222]](#cite_note-Broassim-224)

[British English](/wiki/British_English "British English") is primarily used in Kenya. Additionally, a distinct local dialect, [Kenyan English](/wiki/Kenyan_English "Kenyan English"), is used by some communities and individuals in the country, and contains features unique to it that were derived from local [Bantu languages](/wiki/Bantu_languages "Bantu languages") such as Kiswahili and [Kikuyu](/wiki/Gikuyu_language "Gikuyu language").[[223]](#cite_note-Cliike-225) It has been developing since colonisation and also contains certain elements of [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English"). In January 2026, Kenya was ranked 3rd best English-speaking nation in Africa behind South Africa and Zimbabwe and 9th in the world according to English Proficiency Index (2025). [Sheng](/wiki/Sheng_slang "Sheng slang") is a Kiswahili-based [cant](/wiki/Cant_(language) "Cant (language)") spoken in some urban areas. Primarily a mixture of Swahili and English, it is an example of linguistic [code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching").[[224]](#cite_note-Nphil-226)

69 languages are spoken in Kenya. Most belong to two broad language families: [Niger-Congo](/wiki/Niger-Congo_languages "Niger-Congo languages") ([Bantu branch](/wiki/Bantu_languages "Bantu languages")) and [Nilo-Saharan](/wiki/Nilo-Saharan_languages "Nilo-Saharan languages") ([Nilotic branch](/wiki/Nilotic_languages "Nilotic languages")), spoken by the country's Bantu and Nilotic populations respectively. The Cushitic and Arab ethnic minorities speak languages belonging to the separate [Afroasiatic](/wiki/Afroasiatic_languages "Afroasiatic languages") family, with the Indian and European residents speaking languages from the [Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") family.[[225]](#cite_note-Ethnken-227)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Kenya](/wiki/Religion_in_Kenya "Religion in Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Catholic_Church_in_Mombasa.JPG/250px-Catholic_Church_in_Mombasa.JPG)](/wiki/File:Catholic_Church_in_Mombasa.JPG)

[Holy Ghost Catholic Cathedral](/wiki/Holy_Ghost_Cathedral,_Mombasa "Holy Ghost Cathedral, Mombasa") in Mombasa

Most Kenyans are [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians") (85.5%), with 33.4% [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), 20.6% [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Kenya "Catholic Church in Kenya") and 20.4% [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"). The Catholic Church is the largest single Christian church in Kenya, with about 10 million members.[[2]](#cite_note-Census2019a-2) The [Presbyterian Church of East Africa](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_East_Africa "Presbyterian Church of East Africa") has 3 million followers in Kenya and surrounding countries.[[227]](#cite_note-229) There are smaller conservative [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition") churches, the [Africa Evangelical Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Africa_Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church "Africa Evangelical Presbyterian Church"),[[228]](#cite_note-230) the [Independent Presbyterian Church in Kenya](/wiki/Independent_Presbyterian_Church_in_Kenya "Independent Presbyterian Church in Kenya"), and the [Reformed Church of East Africa](/wiki/Reformed_Church_of_East_Africa "Reformed Church of East Africa"). [Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") has 621,200 adherents.[[229]](#cite_note-231) Kenya has by far the highest number of [Quakers](/wiki/Quaker "Quaker") of any country in the world, with around 146,300.[[230]](#cite_note-232) The only [Jewish synagogue](/wiki/Jewish_synagogue "Jewish synagogue") in the country is in Nairobi.

Islam is the [second largest religion](/wiki/Islam_in_Kenya "Islam in Kenya"), comprising 11% of the population.[[3]](#cite_note-religious-freedom-3) 60% of Kenyan Muslims live in the [Coastal Region](/wiki/Coast_Province "Coast Province"), comprising 50% of the total population there, while the upper part of Kenya's [Eastern Region](/wiki/Eastern_Province_(Kenya) "Eastern Province (Kenya)") is home to 10% of the country's Muslims, where they are the majority religious group.[[231]](#cite_note-233) [Indigenous beliefs](/wiki/African_traditional_religion "African traditional religion") are practised by 0.7% of the population, although many self-identifying Christians and Muslims [maintain some traditional beliefs and customs](/wiki/Religious_syncretism "Religious syncretism"). Nonreligious Kenyans are 1.6% of the population.[[2]](#cite_note-Census2019a-2)

Some Hindus also live in Kenya. The numbers are estimated to be around 60,287, or 0.13% of the population.[[2]](#cite_note-Census2019a-2)

### Health

Main article: [Health in Kenya](/wiki/Health_in_Kenya "Health in Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Kapsowarhospital1.jpg/250px-Kapsowarhospital1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kapsowarhospital1.jpg)

Outpatient Department of AIC Kapsowar Hospital[[232]](#cite_note-234) in [Kapsowar](/wiki/Kapsowar "Kapsowar")

Health care is one of the low-priority sectors in Kenya and was allocated 4.8% of the national budget in 2019/2020 or just 4.59% of [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") compared to high-priority sectors such as education which was allocated more than 25%. This is below the 4.98% average in [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") and 9.83% spent globally.

According to the National and County Health Budget Analysis FY 2020/21, the breakdown of county health expenditure was 58% on Policy Planning and Administrative Support Services, 28% on Curative and Rehabilitative Health Services, 8% on Preventive and Promotive Health Services and 7% on Other Programmes.

Health care is largely funded by private individuals and their families or employers through direct payments to [health care providers](/wiki/Health_care_provider "Health care provider"), to the [National Health Insurance Fund](/wiki/National_Hospital_Insurance_Fund "National Hospital Insurance Fund") or to medical insurance companies. Additional funding comes from local, international and some government [social safety net](/wiki/Social_safety_net "Social safety net") schemes. Public hospitals are [fee-for-service](/wiki/Fee-for-service "Fee-for-service") establishments that generate large amounts of county and national government revenues making them highly political and corrupt enterprises.[[233]](#cite_note-235)

Private health facilities are diverse, highly dynamic, and difficult to classify, unlike public health facilities, which are easily grouped in classes that consist of community-based (level I) services, run by [community health workers](/wiki/Community_health_worker "Community health worker"); dispensaries (level II facilities), run by [nurses](/wiki/Nurse "Nurse"); health centres (level III facilities), run by [clinical officers](/wiki/Clinical_officer "Clinical officer"); sub-county hospitals (level IV facilities), which may be run by a clinical officer or a [medical officer](/wiki/General_practitioner "General practitioner"); county hospitals (level V facilities), which may be run by a medical officer or a [medical practitioner](/wiki/Consultant_(medicine) "Consultant (medicine)"); and national referral hospitals (level VI facilities), which are run by fully qualified medical practitioners.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Table_rco_co_mo_medical_practitioners.png/250px-Table_rco_co_mo_medical_practitioners.png)](/wiki/File:Table_rco_co_mo_medical_practitioners.png)

Table showing different grades of clinical officers, medical officers, and medical practitioners in Kenya's public service

[Nurses](/wiki/Nursing "Nursing") are by far the largest group of [front-line](/wiki/Front-line "Front-line") health care providers in all sectors, followed by clinical officers, medical officers, and medical practitioners. These are absorbed and deployed into government service in accordance with the Scheme of Service for Nursing Personnel (2014), the Revised Scheme of Service for Clinical Personnel (2020) and the Revised Scheme of Service for Medical Officers and Dental Officers (2016).

[Traditional healers](/wiki/Traditional_healer "Traditional healer") ([herbalists](/wiki/Herbalist "Herbalist"), [witch doctors](/wiki/Witch_doctor "Witch doctor"), and [faith healers](/wiki/Faith_healer "Faith healer")) are readily available, trusted, and widely consulted as practitioners of first or last choice by both rural and urban dwellers.

Despite major achievements in the health sector, Kenya still faces many challenges. The estimated [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") dropped in 2009 to approximately 55 years — five years below the 1990 level.[[234]](#cite_note-236) The [infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") rate was high at approximately 44 deaths per 1,000 children in 2012.[[235]](#cite_note-237) The WHO estimated in 2011 that only 42% of births were attended by a skilled health professional.[[236]](#cite_note-238)

[Diseases of poverty](/wiki/Diseases_of_poverty "Diseases of poverty") directly correlate with a country's [economic performance](/wiki/Economy "Economy") and [wealth distribution](/wiki/Wealth_distribution "Wealth distribution"): In 2015/16, 35.6% of Kenyans lived below the poverty line.[[237]](#cite_note-Poverty_Incidence_in_Kenya-239) Preventable diseases like malaria, HIV/AIDS, pneumonia, diarrhoea, and malnutrition are the biggest burden, major child-killers, and responsible for much morbidity; weak policies, corruption, inadequate health workers, weak management, and poor leadership in the public health sector are largely to blame. According to 2009 estimates, [HIV/AIDS prevalence](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_HIV/AIDS_adult_prevalence_rate "List of countries by HIV/AIDS adult prevalence rate") is about 6.3% of the adult population.[[238]](#cite_note-240) However, the 2011 UNAIDS Report suggests that the HIV epidemic may be improving in Kenya, as HIV prevalence is declining among young people (ages 15–24) and pregnant women.[[239]](#cite_note-241) Kenya had an estimated 15 million cases of [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria") in 2006.[[240]](#cite_note-242) [Tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis") is a major public health problem. The per capita incidence of TB in Kenya more than quadrupled between 1990 and 2015.[[241]](#cite_note-243)

The 2024 Global Hunger Index gave Kenya a 25.0 score, indicating that the severity of hunger is "serious".[[242]](#cite_note-244)

### Women

Main articles: [Women in Kenya](/wiki/Women_in_Kenya "Women in Kenya"), [Child marriage in Kenya](/wiki/Child_marriage_in_Kenya "Child marriage in Kenya"), and [Polygamy in Kenya](/wiki/Polygamy_in_Kenya "Polygamy in Kenya")

The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") in Kenya was estimated to be 4.49 children per woman in 2012.[[243]](#cite_note-245) According to a 2008–09 survey by the Kenyan government, the total fertility rate was 4.6% and the contraception usage rate among married women was 46%.[[244]](#cite_note-246) [Maternal mortality](/wiki/Maternal_mortality "Maternal mortality") is high, partly because of [female genital mutilation](/wiki/Female_genital_cutting "Female genital cutting"),[[175]](#cite_note-lcweb2.loc.gov-177) with about 27% of women having undergone it.[[245]](#cite_note-247) However, this practice is on the decline as the country becomes more modernised, and in 2011 it was banned in Kenya.[[246]](#cite_note-248) Women were economically empowered before colonialisation. By colonial land alienation, women lost access and control of land.[[247]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-249) They became more economically dependent on men.[[247]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-249) A colonial order of gender emerged where males dominated females.[[247]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-249)
Median age at first marriage increases with increasing education.[[248]](#cite_note-250)
Rape, defilement, and battering are not always seen as serious crimes.[[249]](#cite_note-auto2-251)
Reports of sexual assault are not always taken seriously.[[249]](#cite_note-auto2-251)

### Youth

Article 260 of the [Kenyan Constitution](/wiki/2010_Kenyan_constitutional_referendum "2010 Kenyan constitutional referendum") of 2010 defines youth as those between the ages of 18 and 34.[[250]](#cite_note-252) According to the 2019 Population and Census results, 75 percent of the 47.6 million population is under the age of 35, making Kenya a country of the [youth](/wiki/Youth "Youth").[[251]](#cite_note-253) Youth unemployment and underemployment in Kenya has become a problem.[[252]](#cite_note-auto3-254) According to the Kenya National Bureau of Statistics (KNBS), approximately 1.7 million people lost their jobs as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, which eliminated some informal jobs and caused the economy to slow.[[252]](#cite_note-auto3-254) The Kenyan government has made progress in addressing the high youth unemployment by implementing various affirmative action programs and projects which include: the [National Youth Service](/wiki/National_Youth_Service_(Kenya) "National Youth Service (Kenya)"), The National Youth Enterprise Development Fund,[[253]](#cite_note-255) The Women Enterprise Fund,[[254]](#cite_note-256) *Kazi Mtaani, Ajira Digital, Kikao Mtaani*,[[255]](#cite_note-257) *Uwezo fund*,[[256]](#cite_note-258) Future Bora[[257]](#cite_note-259) and *Studio mashinani*[[258]](#cite_note-260) that [empower youth](/wiki/Youth_empowerment "Youth empowerment"), offer job opportunities and to raise one's standard of living.

### Education

Main article: [Education in Kenya](/wiki/Education_in_Kenya "Education in Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Community_work_in_Kenya_DVIDS342488.jpg/250px-Community_work_in_Kenya_DVIDS342488.jpg)](/wiki/File:Community_work_in_Kenya_DVIDS342488.jpg)

Schoolchildren in a classroom

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/MSc_student_at_Kenyatta_University.jpg/250px-MSc_student_at_Kenyatta_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:MSc_student_at_Kenyatta_University.jpg)

An MSc student at [Kenyatta University](/wiki/Kenyatta_University "Kenyatta University") in Nairobi

Children attend nursery school, or kindergarten in the private sector, until they are five years old. This lasts one to three years (KG1, KG2 and KG3) and is financed privately because there has been no government policy on pre-schooling until recently.[[259]](#cite_note-261)

Basic formal education starts at the age of six and lasts 12 years, consisting of eight years in primary school and four in high school or secondary. Primary school is free in public schools and those attending can join a vocational youth/village polytechnic, or make their own arrangements for an apprenticeship program and learn a trade such as tailoring, carpentry, motor vehicle repair, brick-laying and masonry for about two years.[[260]](#cite_note-:2-262)

Those who complete high school can join a [polytechnic](/wiki/Institute_of_technology "Institute of technology") or other technical college and study for three years, or proceed directly to university and study for four years. Graduates from the polytechnics and colleges can then join the workforce and later obtain a specialized higher diploma qualification after a further one to two years of training, or join the university—usually in the second or third year of their respective course. The [higher diploma](/wiki/Higher_diploma "Higher diploma") is accepted by many employers in place of a bachelor's degree and direct or accelerated admission to post-graduate studies is possible in some universities.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Masai_girl_at_school_doing_maths.jpg/250px-Masai_girl_at_school_doing_maths.jpg)](/wiki/File:Masai_girl_at_school_doing_maths.jpg)

A Maasai girl at school

Public universities in Kenya are highly commercialized institutions and only a small fraction of qualified high school graduates are admitted on limited government-sponsorship into programs of their choice. Most are admitted into the social sciences, which are cheap to run, or as self-sponsored students paying the full cost of their studies. Most qualified students who miss out opt for middle-level diploma programs in public or private universities, colleges, and polytechnics.

In 2018, 18.5 percent of the Kenyan adult population was illiterate, which was the highest rate of literacy in East Africa.[[261]](#cite_note-263)[[262]](#cite_note-264) There are very wide regional disparities: for example, Nairobi had the highest level of literacy at 87.1 per cent, compared to North Eastern Province, the lowest, at 8.0 per cent. Preschool, which targets children from age three to five, is an integral component of the education system and is a key requirement for admission to Standard One (First Grade). At the end of primary education, pupils sit the [Kenya Certificate of Primary Education](/wiki/Kenya_Certificate_of_Primary_Education "Kenya Certificate of Primary Education") (KCPE), which determines those who proceed to secondary school or vocational training. The result of this examination is needed for placement at secondary school.[[260]](#cite_note-:2-262)

Primary school is for students aged 6/7-13/14 years. For those who proceed to the secondary level, there is a national examination at the end of Form Four – the [Kenya Certificate of Secondary Education](/wiki/Kenya_Certificate_of_Secondary_Education "Kenya Certificate of Secondary Education") (KCSE), which determines those proceeding to the universities, other professional training, or employment. Students sit [examinations](/wiki/Examinations "Examinations") in eight subjects of their choosing. However, English, Kiswahili, and mathematics are compulsory subjects.

The Kenya Universities and Colleges Central Placement Service (KUCCPS), formerly the Joint Admissions Board (JAB), is responsible for selecting students joining the public universities. Other than the public schools, there are many private schools, mainly in urban areas. Similarly, there are a number of [international schools](/wiki/International_school "International school") catering to various overseas educational systems.

Kenya was ranked 102nd in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[263]](#cite_note-265)[[264]](#cite_note-266)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Kenya](/wiki/Culture_of_Kenya "Culture of Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Kenyan_dancers.jpg/250px-Kenyan_dancers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kenyan_dancers.jpg)

Kenyan boys and girls performing a traditional dance

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Nation_media_house.jpg/250px-Nation_media_house.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nation_media_house.jpg)

Nation Media House, which hosts the [Nation Media Group](/wiki/Nation_Media_Group "Nation Media Group")

The [culture of Kenya](/wiki/Culture_of_Kenya "Culture of Kenya") comprises multiple traditions from 42 (+) tribes.[[265]](#cite_note-267) Kenya has no single prominent culture. It instead consists of the various cultures of the country's different communities.

Notable populations include the [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_people "Swahili people") on the coast, several other [Bantu](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") communities in the central and western regions, and Nilotic communities in the northwest. The [Maasai](/wiki/Maasai_people "Maasai people") culture is well known to tourism, despite constituting a relatively small part of Kenya's population. They are renowned for their elaborate upper-body adornment and jewellery.

Kenya has an extensive music, television, and theatre scene.

### Literature

Main article: [Kenyan literature](/wiki/Kenyan_literature "Kenyan literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Ng%C5%A9g%C4%A9_wa_Thiong%27o_%28signing_autographs_in_London%29.jpg/250px-Ng%C5%A9g%C4%A9_wa_Thiong%27o_%28signing_autographs_in_London%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ng%C5%A9g%C4%A9_wa_Thiong%27o_(signing_autographs_in_London).jpg)

Kenyan author Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o

[Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o](/wiki/Ng%C5%A9g%C4%A9_wa_Thiong%27o "Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o") is one of Kenya's best-known writers. His novel *[Weep Not, Child](/wiki/Weep_Not,_Child "Weep Not, Child")* depicts life in Kenya during the British occupation. The story details the effects of the Mau Mau on the lives of Kenyans. Its combination of themes—[colonialism](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism"), education, and love—helped make it one of the best-known African novels.

[M.G. Vassanji](/wiki/M.G._Vassanji "M.G. Vassanji")'s 2003 novel *The In-Between World of Vikram Lall* won the [Giller Prize](/wiki/Scotiabank_Giller_Prize "Scotiabank Giller Prize") in 2003. It is the fictional memoir of a Kenyan of Indian heritage and his family as they adjust to the changing political climates in colonial and post-colonial Kenya.

Since 2003, the literary journal *[Kwani?](/wiki/Kwani%3F "Kwani?")* has been publishing Kenyan contemporary literature. Kenya has also nurtured emerging versatile authors such as Paul Kipchumba (Kipwendui, Kibiwott) who demonstrate a pan-African outlook.[[266]](#cite_note-268)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Kenya](/wiki/Music_of_Kenya "Music of Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Juacali_1.jpg/250px-Juacali_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Juacali_1.jpg)

Popular Kenyan musician Jua Cali

Kenya has a diverse assortment of popular music forms, in addition to multiple types of [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") based on the variety of over 40 regional languages.[[267]](#cite_note-269)

Drums are the most dominant instrument in popular Kenyan music. Drum beats are very complex and include both native rhythms and imported ones, especially the [Congolese](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") [cavacha](/wiki/Cavacha "Cavacha") rhythm. Popular Kenyan music usually involves the interplay of multiple parts, and more recently, showy guitar solos as well. There are also a number of local hip-hop artists, including [Jua Cali](/wiki/Jua_Cali "Jua Cali"); Afro-pop bands such as [Sauti Sol](/wiki/Sauti_Sol "Sauti Sol"); and musicians who play local genres like Benga, such as [Akothee](/wiki/Akothee "Akothee").

Lyrics are most often in Kiswahili or English. There is also some emerging aspect of [Lingala](/wiki/Lingala_language "Lingala language") borrowed from [Congolese](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") musicians. Lyrics are also written in local languages. Urban radio generally only plays English music, though there also exist a number of vernacular radio stations.

*Zilizopendwa* is a genre of local urban music that was recorded in the 1960s, 70s, and 80s by musicians such as [Daudi Kabaka](/wiki/Daudi_Kabaka "Daudi Kabaka"), [Fadhili William](/wiki/Fadhili_William "Fadhili William"), and Sukuma Bin Ongaro, and is particularly enjoyed by older people—having been popularised by the Kenya Broadcasting Corporation's Kiswahili service (formerly called Voice of Kenya or VOK).

The Isukuti is a vigorous dance performed by the [Luhya](/wiki/Luhya_people "Luhya people") sub-tribes to the beat of a traditional drum called the Isukuti during many occasions such as the birth of a child, marriage, or funeral. Other traditional dances include the [Ohangla](/wiki/Ohangla "Ohangla") among the [Luo](/wiki/Luo_peoples "Luo peoples"), Nzele among the [Mijikenda](/wiki/Mijikenda_peoples "Mijikenda peoples"), [Mugithi](/wiki/Mugithi "Mugithi") among the [Kikuyu](/wiki/Kikuyu_people "Kikuyu people"), and [Taarab](/wiki/Taarab "Taarab") among the [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_people "Swahili people").

Kenya has a growing Christian gospel music scene. Prominent local gospel musicians include the [Kenyan Boys Choir](/wiki/Kenyan_Boys_Choir "Kenyan Boys Choir").

[Benga music](/wiki/Benga_music "Benga music") has been popular since the late 1960s, especially in the area around [Lake Victoria](/wiki/Lake_Victoria "Lake Victoria"). The word *benga* is occasionally used to refer to any kind of pop music. Bass, guitar, and percussion are the usual instruments.

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Kenya](/wiki/Sport_in_Kenya "Sport in Kenya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/2012_Olympics_-_Womens_5000m_start_4.jpg/250px-2012_Olympics_-_Womens_5000m_start_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:2012_Olympics_-_Womens_5000m_start_4.jpg)

Jepkosgei Kipyego and Jepkemoi Cheruiyot at the 2012 London Olympics

Kenya is active in several sports, among them [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), [rallying](/wiki/Rallying "Rallying"), [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"), [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"), and [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"). The country is known chiefly for its dominance in [middle-distance](/wiki/Middle-distance_running "Middle-distance running") and [long-distance](/wiki/Long-distance_running "Long-distance running") athletics, having consistently produced [Olympic](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") and [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") champions in various distance events, especially in 800 m, 1,500 m, 3,000 m steeplechase, 5,000 m, 10,000 m, and the marathon. Kenyan athletes (particularly [Kalenjin](/wiki/Kalenjin_people "Kalenjin people")), continue to dominate the world of distance running, although competition from [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") and [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia") has reduced this supremacy. Some of Kenya's best-known athletes include the four-time women's [Boston Marathon](/wiki/Boston_Marathon "Boston Marathon") winner and two-time world champion [Catherine Ndereba](/wiki/Catherine_Ndereba "Catherine Ndereba"), 800m world record holder [David Rudisha](/wiki/David_Rudisha "David Rudisha"), former [marathon](/wiki/Marathon_(sport) "Marathon (sport)") world record-holder [Paul Tergat](/wiki/Paul_Tergat "Paul Tergat"), and 5000m Olympic gold medalist [John Ngugi](/wiki/John_Ngugi "John Ngugi"). Kenya's most decorated athlete is three-time Olympic gold medalist and eleven-time world marathon major champion, [Eliud Kipchoge](/wiki/Eliud_Kipchoge "Eliud Kipchoge").

Kenya won several medals during the Beijing Olympics: six gold, four silver, and four bronze, making it Africa's most successful nation in the 2008 Olympics. New athletes gained attention, such as [Pamela Jelimo](/wiki/Pamela_Jelimo "Pamela Jelimo"), the women's 800m gold medalist who went on to win the [IAAF Golden League](/wiki/IAAF_Golden_League "IAAF Golden League") jackpot, and [Samuel Wanjiru](/wiki/Samuel_Wanjiru "Samuel Wanjiru"), who won the men's marathon. Retired Olympic and Commonwealth Games champion [Kipchoge Keino](/wiki/Kipchoge_Keino "Kipchoge Keino") helped usher in Kenya's ongoing distance dynasty in the 1970s and was followed by Commonwealth Champion [Henry Rono](/wiki/Henry_Rono "Henry Rono")'s spectacular string of world record performances. Lately, there has been controversy in Kenyan athletics circles, with the defection of a number of Kenyan athletes to represent other countries, chiefly [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain") and [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar").[[268]](#cite_note-IAAF-270) The Kenyan Ministry of Sports has tried to stop the defections, but they have continued anyway, with [Bernard Lagat](/wiki/Bernard_Lagat "Bernard Lagat") being the latest, choosing to represent the United States.[[268]](#cite_note-IAAF-270) Most of these defections occur because of economic or financial factors.[[269]](#cite_note-271) Decisions by the Kenyan government to tax athletes' earnings may also be a motivating factor.[[270]](#cite_note-272) Some elite Kenyan runners who cannot qualify for their country's strong national team find it easier to qualify by running for other countries.[[271]](#cite_note-273)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/David_Rudisha_Daegu_2011.jpg/250px-David_Rudisha_Daegu_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:David_Rudisha_Daegu_2011.jpg)

Kenyan Olympic and world record holder in the 800 metres, [David Rudisha](/wiki/David_Rudisha "David Rudisha")

Kenya has been a dominant force in women's volleyball within Africa, with both the clubs and the national team winning various continental championships in the past decade.[[272]](#cite_note-274)[[273]](#cite_note-275) The women's team has competed at the Olympics and [World Championships](/wiki/FIVB_Volleyball_Women%27s_World_Championship "FIVB Volleyball Women's World Championship"), though without any notable success. Cricket is another popular sport, also ranking as the most successful team sport. [Kenya](/wiki/Kenyan_cricket_team "Kenyan cricket team") has competed in the [Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup") since [1996](/wiki/1996_Cricket_World_Cup "1996 Cricket World Cup"). They upset some of the world's best teams and reached the semi-finals of the [2003 tournament](/wiki/2003_Cricket_World_Cup "2003 Cricket World Cup"). They won the inaugural World Cricket League Division 1 hosted in Nairobi and participated in the World T20. They also participated in the [ICC Cricket World Cup 2011](/wiki/ICC_Cricket_World_Cup_2011 "ICC Cricket World Cup 2011"). Their current captain is [Rakep Patel](/wiki/Rakep_Patel "Rakep Patel").[[274]](#cite_note-276)

Rugby is increasing in popularity, especially with the annual Safari Sevens tournament. The [Kenya Sevens team](/wiki/Kenya_national_rugby_sevens_team "Kenya national rugby sevens team") ranked 9th in the IRB Sevens World Series for the 2006 season. In 2016, the team beat Fiji at the Singapore Sevens finals, making Kenya the second African nation after South Africa to win a World Series championship.[[275]](#cite_note-277)[[276]](#cite_note-278)[[277]](#cite_note-279) [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya_national_football_team "Kenya national football team") was once also a regional powerhouse in football. However, its dominance has been eroded by wrangles within the now defunct [Kenya Football Federation](/wiki/Kenya_Football_Federation "Kenya Football Federation"),[[278]](#cite_note-280) leading to a suspension by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") which was lifted in March 2007.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the motor rallying arena, Kenya is home to the world-famous [Safari Rally](/wiki/Safari_Rally "Safari Rally"), commonly acknowledged as one of the toughest rallies in the world.[[279]](#cite_note-281) First held in 1953, it was a part of the [World Rally Championship](/wiki/World_Rally_Championship "World Rally Championship") for many years until its exclusion after the 2002 event owing to financial difficulties. Some of the best rally drivers in the world have taken part in and won the rally, such as [Björn Waldegård](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rn_Waldeg%C3%A5rd "Björn Waldegård"), [Hannu Mikkola](/wiki/Hannu_Mikkola "Hannu Mikkola"), [Tommi Mäkinen](/wiki/Tommi_M%C3%A4kinen "Tommi Mäkinen"), [Shekhar Mehta](/wiki/Shekhar_Mehta "Shekhar Mehta"), [Carlos Sainz](/wiki/Carlos_Sainz_Sr. "Carlos Sainz Sr."), and [Colin McRae](/wiki/Colin_McRae "Colin McRae"). The Safari Rally returned to the world championship in [2021](/wiki/2021_Safari_Rally "2021 Safari Rally"), after the 2003–2019 events ran as part of the [African Rally Championship](/wiki/African_Rally_Championship "African Rally Championship").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Nairobi has hosted several major continental sports events, including the [FIBA Africa Championship 1993](/wiki/FIBA_Africa_Championship_1993 "FIBA Africa Championship 1993"), where [Kenya's national basketball team](/wiki/Kenya_national_basketball_team "Kenya national basketball team") finished in the top four, its best performance to date.[[280]](#cite_note-282)

Kenya also has its own [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team, the Kenya Ice Lions.[[281]](#cite_note-283) The team's home ground is the [Solar Ice Rink](/wiki/Solar_Ice_Rink "Solar Ice Rink") at the Panari Sky Centre in Nairobi,[[282]](#cite_note-284)[[283]](#cite_note-285) which is the first and largest [ice rink](/wiki/Ice_rink "Ice rink") in all of Africa.[[284]](#cite_note-286)

[Kenya men's national field hockey team](/wiki/Kenya_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Kenya men's national field hockey team") was considered one of the good teams in the world during 1960s and 1970s.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Roshan_Ali.jpg/250px-Roshan_Ali.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roshan_Ali.jpg)

Roshan Ali, former goalkeeper of Kenya field hockey team

Kenya took 6th position in [1964 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics") hockey tournament and 4th in 1971 [Men's FIH Hockey World Cup](/wiki/Men%27s_FIH_Hockey_World_Cup "Men's FIH Hockey World Cup").

### Cuisine

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Ugali_%26_Sukuma_Wiki.jpg/250px-Ugali_%26_Sukuma_Wiki.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ugali_%26_Sukuma_Wiki.jpg)

[Ugali](/wiki/Ugali "Ugali") and [sukuma wiki](/wiki/Sukuma_wiki "Sukuma wiki"), staples of Kenyan cuisine

Kenyans generally have three meals in a day—breakfast (*kiamsha kinywa*), lunch (*chakula cha mchana*), and supper (*chakula cha jioni* or simply *chajio*). In between, they have the 10-o'clock tea (*chai ya saa nne*) and 4 p.m. tea (*chai ya saa kumi*). Breakfast is usually tea or porridge with bread, [chapati](/wiki/Chapati "Chapati"), [mahamri](/wiki/Mahamri "Mahamri"), boiled sweet potatoes, or [yams](/wiki/Yam_(vegetable) "Yam (vegetable)"). [Githeri](/wiki/Githeri "Githeri") is a common lunchtime dish in many households, while [Ugali](/wiki/Ugali "Ugali") with vegetables, sour milk ([mursik](/wiki/Mursik "Mursik")), meat, fish, or any other stew is generally eaten by much of the population for lunch or supper. Regional variations and dishes also exist.

In western Kenya, among the [Luo](/wiki/Luo_(Kenya_and_Tanzania) "Luo (Kenya and Tanzania)"), fish is a common dish; among the [Kalenjin](/wiki/Kalenjin_people "Kalenjin people"), who dominate much of the Rift Valley Region, mursik—sour milk—is a common drink.

In cities such as Nairobi, there are fast-food restaurants, including [Steers](/wiki/Steers_(restaurant) "Steers (restaurant)"), [KFC](/wiki/KFC "KFC"),[[285]](#cite_note-287) and [Subway](/wiki/Subway_(restaurant) "Subway (restaurant)").[[286]](#cite_note-288) There are also many fish-and-chips shops.[[287]](#cite_note-289)

[Cheese](/wiki/Cheese_in_Kenya "Cheese in Kenya") is becoming more popular in Kenya, with consumption increasing particularly among the middle class.[[288]](#cite_note-290)[[289]](#cite_note-291)
